Introduction
Low back pain (LBP) is a widespread condition that affects at 
least 80% of people at some point in their lives. It ranks second 
only to the common cold as the leading cause for visits to pri -
mary care physicians and is managed at every level of the health 
care system, from emergency rooms to community care set -
tings.349 LBP refers to discomfort, pain, muscle tension, or stiff -
ness in the lumbosacral region, typically defined as the area  
between the lower ribcage and the crease of the buttocks. LBP 
often extends or radiates into one or both legs. LBP can vary 
widely in severity, ranging from mild, occasional discomfort to 
severe, debilitating pain. Fortunately, most cases of LBP resolve 
quickly and without significant limitation. However, when LBP 
becomes chronic, it can lead to morbidity, reduced quality of life, 
and increased health care costs. This chapter will explore the 
anatomy and physiology of the lumbar spine and examine the 
common causes of LBP . T reatment modalities will also be re -
viewed, including interventional approaches to low back and 
radicular pain. Some serious but less common causes of LBP will 
not be addressed because they require diagnosis and targeted 
management of the specific pathology; the topics not covered in 
this chapter include referred pain from visceral disorders, can -
cers, infections, and traumatic fractures. 
Prognosis of Low Back Pain
For most, an acute episode of LBP is self-limiting, and the major-
ity do not seek medical care.376 New acute episodes of LBP gener-
ally have a favorable prognosis, with 75% to 90% of individuals 
recovering from pain and disability or returning to work within  
3 to 4 weeks. 141 For those who do seek medical attention, the 
typical course of acute LBP is initially favorable. A systematic  
review of 33 prospective cohort studies ( n 5 11,166) concluded 
that most patients presenting to the primary care setting with acute 
or persistent LBP improved rapidly within the first 6 weeks. After  
6 weeks, the pace of recovery slowed, with only gradual reductions 
in pain and disability over the course of a year. At the 1-year mark, 
individuals with acute back pain generally reported low levels of 
pain and disability, while those with persistent back pain experi -
ence moderate levels.67 Another systematic review ( n 5 17,974) 
and meta-analysis (n 5 9224) also supported these findings and 
further suggested that the subset of participants with persistent 
LBP had high levels of disability and pain with minimal improve-
ments over time. 367 Therefore identifying and providing early 
intervention for individuals with subacute LBP may be critical  
in preventing the progression to chronic LBP .367 Notably, the defi-
nitions for acute, subacute, and chronic LBP vary in time frame 
with US government agencies such as the Centers for Disease  
be needed when red flags are present or if symptoms persist 
without improvement.
• First-line treatment for acute and subacute LBP includes  
patient education, physical therapy, activity modification, and 
pharmacologic interventions
• Psychological factors, including depression, anxiety, and catastro-
phizing, should be addressed to prevent chronification of pain.
• Interventional pain procedures may be considered for  
patients who do not respond to conservative treatment, 
though evidence for long-term effectiveness varies.
• Surgery may be indicated in cases of significant or progressive 
neurologic deficits, cauda equina syndrome, or when conserva -
tive measures fail.
• Chronic LBP management should adopt an interdisciplinary ap-
proach, including physical, psychological, and social components.
34
Low Back Disorders
SANJOG S. PANGARKAR, MELISSA E. PHUPHANICH, AND UDAI NANDA
• Low back pain (LBP) is the most common cause of disability 
worldwide.
• LBP may arise from various structures, including discs, verte -
brae, muscles, ligaments, and nerves, with both mechanical  
and nonmechanical causes.
• In LBP , which may be classified as acute, subacute, or chronic, it 
is important to distinguish between radiculopathy, nonspecific 
LBP , and specific sources of LBP .
• A comprehensive patient history and physical examination are 
essential, and recognizing red flags helps identify serious  
underlying conditions such as infections or cancers that require 
urgent attention.
• Routine imaging is not recommended in most cases of acute 
nonspecific LBP , but imaging modalities, such as x-rays, mag-
netic resonance images, and computed tomography scans, may

Control and Prevention and the Agency for Healthcare Research 
and Quality using 0 to 4 weeks for acute LBP , 4 to 12 weeks for 
subacute LBP , and greater than 12 weeks for chronic LBP .
Research on the natural history and prognosis of LBP has 
outlined three main trajectory patterns: (1) a recovery trajectory , 
in which the condition improves to little or no pain, (2) an ongo-
ing trajectory , in which the patient has moderate or fluctuating 
pain, and (3) a persistent trajectory , in which the patient has 
constant and severe pain. 187 The majority (approximately 70%) 
of patients with acute LBP fall into the recovery trajectory. In 
contrast, the minority (approximately 30%) of patients with 
chronic LBP fall into the recovery trajectory. 50 Initial high pain 
intensity scores, psychological distress, and concurrent pain in 
multiple areas of the body increase the risk of persistent dis -
abling LBP .138
Risk Factors for Chronic,  
Debilitating Low Back Pain
While most individuals do not develop chronic, debilitating LBP , 
there is a minority who do contribute significantly to its economic 
burden. In fact, 80% to 90% of the health care and social costs 
due to back pain are attributed to the 10% who develop chronic 
pain and disability.138 The majority of these costs come from in -
direct factors, such as work-related loss of productivity, as LBP 
forces more people out of the workplace than any other chronic 
condition.120,179,305 Notably, unlike other medical conditions, risk 
factors for chronic, disabling back pain are not necessarily linked 
to its diagnosis or underlying cause (Box 34.1).53,120,184 The stron-
gest predictors of persistent, disabling LBP are maladaptive pain 
coping behaviors, comorbid psychiatric conditions, nonorganic 
symptoms, severe functional impairment, and poor overall health 
status.53 Disabling LBP is overrepresented in patients with low 
socioeconomic status. Additionally, patients with LBP often expe-
rience pain in other areas of the body, along with physical and 
mental health issues compared to those without back pain. This 
combination of comorbidities, along with chronic, debilitating 
LBP , typically lead to increased medical care and less effective 
treatment outcomes, further escalating the financial burden of the 
condition.138
Epidemiology and Global Health Perspective
LBP is a costly burden to society and the leading cause of disabil-
ity in the United States and worldwide. Globally, LBP is the top 
cause of years lived with disability (YLD), with more than  
0.5 billion cases of LBP worldwide. LBP is more prevalent among 
females compared to males across all ages. The prevalence of LBP 
and YLD also increases with age, with peak prevalence rates ob -
served at approximately 85 years of age. 120 Notably, geriatric 
populations are more likely to develop back pain that is severe and 
chronic and disrupts social and physical functioning. 82 Elderly 
patients are more likely to have poor outcomes, slower recoveries, 
and difficulties caring for themselves.294,295 T otal disability burden 
and disease-related costs are expected to substantially increase as 
the population ages, with more than 800 million people globally 
projected to have LBP by 2050.120
The financial cost, health care utilization, and disability related 
to LBP are highly variable among countries and are substantially 
influenced by sociocultural factors and legislation. For example, 
the economic burden of LBP is approximated to be over $3 billion 
in the United Kingdom and Australia; however, the annual expen-
diture in the United States is estimated to exceed $100 bil -
lion.120,170,184,306 The 2021 Global Burden of Disease (GBD) study 
identified high body mass index (BMI), smoking, and occupa -
tional ergonomic factors as significant risk factors for YLD due to 
LBP . Nearly one-fourth of these YLD were linked to occupational 
factors, such as heavy lifting, forceful movements, bending, twist-
ing, prolonged sitting, or awkward postures.
Notably, the 2021 GBD study did not account for the impact 
of the COVID-19 pandemic on the prevalence and burden of 
LBP , which likely exacerbated occupational ergonomic factors.120 
Since the pandemic, telework and telehealth have become signifi-
cantly more common, with remote work potentially playing a 
major role in work-related LBP . T elework often promotes seden-
tary behavior, extended work hours with fewer breaks, social isola-
tion, limited support from colleagues, poor home workstation 
ergonomics, and a blurred work-life balance. 239 These physical 
and mental health challenges associated with remote work can 
contribute to the development of LBP .
Anatomy and Biomechanics of the  
Lumbar Spine
General Anatomic Concepts
The vertebral column performs several essential functions, includ-
ing stabilizing the appendicular skeleton, safeguarding the neural 
elements, and enabling multiplanar movement. The inherent 
strength of the vertebral column is derived from the size and  
arrangement of each vertebra and the support of the surrounding 
muscles and ligaments. In contrast, the spine’s flexibility is attrib-
uted to a series of closely spaced joints arranged in series, which 
form what is commonly referred to as a three-joint complex. This 
complex consists of the intervertebral discs (IVDs), vertebral end 
plates, and two zygapophyseal joints. Further, the typical lordotic 
curvature of the lumbar spine enhances flexibility and augments 
the spine’s ability to absorb shock.
Vertebrae
The lumbar spine’s bony anatomy consists of five lumbar verte -
brae that include the vertebral body (VB), neural arch, and poste-
rior elements ( Fig. 34.1). Research supports that 3% to 35% of

the population has a transitional vertebra with either a fifth sacral-
ized vertebra or a lumbarized first sacral segment, often referred to 
as L6.206 The VBs increase in size from L1 to L5, with the lower 
three lumbar vertebrae typically being taller along the anterior 
aspect of the column. This creates a wedge-shaped configuration 
that contributes to lumbar lordosis. The structure of the VBs and 
IVDs function together to withstand axially directed loads.
Each side of the bony neural arch is comprised of a pedicle, a 
thick pillar connecting the posterior elements to the VB, which 
are designed to resist bending and transmit forces between the 
VBs and posterior elements. The posterior elements are comprised 
of the laminae, articular processes, and spinous processes, whereas 
the superior and inferior articular processes of each adjacent ver -
tebrae create the zygapophyseal joints. The pars interarticularis is 
the segment of lamina between the superior and inferior articular 
processes (Fig. 34.2) and is often the site of stress fractures (spon-
dylolysis). These fractures may occur as the forces transmitted by 
the vertically oriented lamina shift direction into the horizontally 
oriented pedicle.
30
Intervertebral Discs
The IVDs and their attachments to the vertebral end plates are 
considered a secondary cartilaginous joint, or symphysis. The 
disc consists of the internal nucleus pulposus and the outer  
annulus fibrosus. The nucleus pulposus is the gelatinous inner 
section of the disc and consists of water, proteoglycans, and col -
lagen. The nucleus pulposus is 90% water at birth and derived 
from primitive notochord cells.
230,284 With time, the IVD desic -
cates, causing degenerative changes to develop and structural 
changes to occur within the spine. The annulus fibrosus consists 
of concentric layers of fibers arranged at angles to one another, 
helping to withstand stress from any direction. The outer fibers 
of the annulus have more collagen but less proteoglycans and 
water than the inner fibers.
23 This varying composition supports 
the functional role of the outer fibers to resist flexion, extension, 
rotation, and distraction.
The primary function of the IVD is to absorb shock  
(Fig. 34.3). The annulus fibrosus is the principal shock absorber 
of the spine as the liquid properties of the nucleus render it  
incompressible. When an axial load occurs, the increase in force in 
the nucleus transmits to the annulus fibrosis and stretches its  
fibers. If the fibers break, the result is a herniated nucleus pulposus.

• Fig. 34.3 The mechanism of weight transmission in an intervertebral disc. 
(A) Compression increases the pressure in the nucleus pulposus. This is 
exerted radially onto the annulus fibrosus, and the tension in the annulus 
increases. (B) The tension in the annulus is exerted on the nucleus, pre -
venting it from expanding radially. Nuclear pressure is then exerted on the 
vertebral end plates. (C) Weight is borne, in part, by the annulus fibrosus 
and by the nucleus pulposus. (D) The radial pressure in the nucleus braces 
the annulus, and the pressure on the end plates transmits the load from 
one vertebra to the next. (Modified from Bogduk N, ed. The inter-body joint 
and the intervertebral discs. In: Clinical and Radiological Anatomy of the 
Lumbar Spine and Sacrum. 5th ed. Churchill Livingstone; 2012.)

Because flexion loads the anterior disc, the nucleus is displaced 
posteriorly.191 If the forces are significant, the nucleus may herni-
ate through the posterior annular fibers. However, because the 
lateral fibers of the posterior longitudinal ligament are thinnest, 
posterolateral disc herniations are the most common ( Fig. 34.4). 
The posterolateral portion of the disc is most at risk when forward 
flexion is accompanied by lateral bending (i.e., bending and twist-
ing). The zygapophyseal joints cannot resist rotation when the 
spine is in flexion, thereby increasing torsional shear forces, which 
puts the discs at risk for injury.
The activity of the lumbar muscles correlates well with intra -
discal pressures (i.e., when back muscles contract, there is an  
associated increase in disc pressure). These pressures change  
depending on spinal posture and the activity undertaken. Fig. 34.5 
demonstrates the changes in L3 disc pressure under various posi -
tions and exercises.
245,246 Adding rotation to the already flexed 
posture increases the disc pressure substantially. Comparing lift -
ing maneuvers, there is not a substantial difference in disc pres -
sure when lifting with the legs (i.e., with the back straight and 
knees bent) vs. lifting with the back (i.e., with a forward-flexed 
back and straight legs).
9,10 What decreases the forces on the lum -
bar spine is lifting the load close to the body. Conversely, the 
farther the load is from the chest, the greater the stress on the 
lumbar spine.

Facet Joints
The facet joints (also known as zygapophyseal or Z joints) are 
paired synovial joints that include a synovium and capsule  
(Fig. 34.6). The alignment of the facet joints and direction of  
articulation determine the direction of motion of the adjacent 
vertebrae. The lumbar facet joints lie in the sagittal plane and 
primarily allow flexion and extension. Some lateral bending  
occurs at the joint, but its alignment restricts rotation, which  
reduces torsional stress on the lumbar disc. Rotation is more of a 
component of thoracic spine motion, whereas spinal flexion and 
extension (90%) primarily occur at the L4-L5 and L5-S1 levels.
Ligaments
The two main sets of ligaments of the lumbar spine are the longi-
tudinal and segmental ligaments. The longitudinal ligaments  
include the anterior and posterior longitudinal ligaments, which are 
named according to their position on the VB. The anterior longitu-
dinal ligament acts to resist extension, translation, and rotation.

699Chapter 34  Low Back Disorders
The posterior longitudinal ligament acts to resist flexion. Disrup-
tion of either ligament typically occurs with rotational forces 
rather than flexion or extension. The anterior longitudinal liga -
ment is the strongest and largest of the spinal ligaments. The main 
segmental ligament is the ligamentum flavum (LF), which is a 
paired structure joining adjacent laminae. The LF is a strong  
ligament, which happens to be elastic enough to allow flexion. 
Flexing the lumbar spine puts this ligament on stretch, decreasing 
its redundancy and making it easier to pierce during procedures. 
The other segmental ligaments are the supraspinous, interspinous, 
and intertransverse ligaments. The supraspinous ligaments join 
the tips of adjacent spinous processes and act to resist flexion. The 
segmental ligaments, along with the LF and facet joints, act to 
restrain motion and prevent excessive shear forces during forward 
bending.
29
Muscles
Muscles with Origins on the Lumbar Spine.  These muscles 
can anatomically be subdivided into the posterior and the anterior 
muscles. The posterior muscles include the latissimus dorsi and 
paraspinals. The lumbar paraspinals consist of the chief extensors 
of the spine and the deep layer (rotators and multifidi) (Figs. 34.7 
and 34.8). The multifidi are smaller segmental stabilizers that act 
to control lumbar flexion. They may also provide spinal proprio -
ception given the predominance of muscle spindles seen histo -
logically. The anterior muscles of the lumbar spine include the 
psoas and quadratus lumborum. Because of the direct attachment 
of the psoas to the lumbar spine, tightening of this muscle

accentuates lumbar lordosis, which can increase forces on the 
posterior elements and contribute to zygapophyseal joint pain. 
The quadratus lumborum acts during side bending and can assist 
in lumbar flexion.
Abdominal Musculature.  The superficial abdominal muscles 
include the rectus abdominis and external obliques ( Fig. 34.9A). 
The rectus abdominus helps flex the trunk, while the external 
obliques allow rotation, side bending, and anterior flexion of the 
spine. The deep layer consists of the internal obliques and the 
transversus abdominis (see Fig. 34.9B). The internal obliques are 
responsible for flexion, lateral flexion, and rotation. The transver-
sus abdominus is the deepest layer of the abdominal muscles and 
helps stabilize the spine and pelvis along with assisting in ipsilat -
eral trunk rotation.
Thoracolumbar Fascia.  The thoracolumbar fascia, with its  
attachments to the transversus abdominis and the internal 
obliques, acts as an abdominal and lumbar brace, particularly 
when lifting. This abdominal bracing mechanism results from 
contraction of these deep abdominal muscles, which creates ten -
sion in the thoracolumbar fascia.
Pelvic Stabilizers. The pelvic stabilizers are included in the 
discussion despite not having direct attachment to the lumbar 
spine because of their influence on movement. The gluteus  
medius stabilizes the pelvis during gait. Weakness or inhibition of 
this muscle results in pelvic instability, thereby introducing lum -
bar side bending and rotation, which can create increased shear or 
torsion on the lumbar discs. The piriformis is an external rotator 
of the hip and sacrum; when tight, it can result in increased shear

Nerves
The conus medullaris is the terminal end of the spinal cord and 
typically ends at the first or second lumbar vertebra. It is followed 
by the cauda equina, which consists of the dorsal and ventral root-
lets. These rootlets join together in the intervertebral neuroforamen 
to become the spinal nerves (Fig. 34.10). The spinal nerve gives off 
the ventral primary ramus. The ventral primary rami from multiple 
levels form the lumbar and lumbosacral plexus to innervate the 
lower limbs. The dorsal primary ramus, with its three branches 
(medial, intermediate, and lateral), innervates the posterior half of

31
Pain Generators of the Lumbar and  
Sacral Spine
The low back is an anatomically diverse set of structures with 
many potential sources of pain. One strategy to clarify these  
potential sources is by learning which anatomic structures are  
innervated and capable of causing pain symptoms (Box 34.2).
Intervertebral Discs
The sinuvertebral nerve innervates the external annulus fibrosus, 
which has the potential for transmitting pain signals. The internal 
annulus fibrosus and nucleus pulposus do not have innervation and 
therefore, in nondisease states, should not transmit pain signals.

Facet Joints
The medial branch of the dorsal primary ramus innervates the 
facet joints, interspinous ligaments, and lumbar multifidi, all of 
which can transmit pain signals.
Spinal Ligaments
The sinuvertebral nerve innervates the posterior longitudinal liga-
ment. The posterior longitudinal ligament is a highly innervated 
structure and can play an important role in LBP perception with 
lumbar disc herniations. The anterior longitudinal ligament is  
innervated by the gray rami communicans, which branches off  
the lumbar sympathetic chain and can transmit pain.
Vertebral Body
The sinuvertebral nerve innervates the anterior VB, and small 
branches of the dorsal primary ramus innervate the posterior VB. 
Although previously thought to be asymptomatic, vertebral com-
pression fractures are now an accepted cause of back pain.
100
Sacroiliac Joint
The innervation of the sacroiliac joint is typically from the ventral 
rami of L4 and L5, the superior gluteal nerve, and the dorsal rami 
of L5, S1, and S2, but may vary from patient to patient. The 
sacroiliac joint is a known transmitter of pain signals.
Concepts of Low Back Pain
The Aging Spine: A Degenerative Cascade
Kirkaldy-Willis et al. have supplied us with a comprehensive the-
ory describing the cascade of events in degenerative lumbar spine 
disease that results in disc herniations, spondylosis, and eventually 
multilevel spinal stenosis.
182 At the heart of this theory is that, 
although the facet joints and the anterior IVDs are separated ana-
tomically, conditions affecting one alter and affect the other. For 
example, axial compression injuries can damage the vertebral end 
plates, which can lead to degenerative disc disease. This eventually 
stresses the zygapophyseal joints, leading to common degenerative 
changes seen over time. T orsional stress can injure the facet joints 
and the discs, which in turn leads to increased stress on both of 
these elements.
By studying multiple magnetic resonance images (MRIs) of 
aging spines, disc degeneration is typically seen first and can pre -
cede facet joint disease by as much as 20 years.
114 When these 
degenerative changes occur at a single level, a cascade of events 
may follow, placing stress on the levels above and below the  
affected level. This eventually results in more generalized and 
multilevel spondylosis in the spine. T o simplify discussion of the  
degenerative cascade, we will separate our discussion of the 
changes that occur in the zygapophyseal joints from those in  
the disc, realizing that they can occur simultaneously and affect 
each other (Fig. 34.12).
T ears in the annulus are thought to be the first anatomic sign of 
degenerative change. When the annulus is weakened enough, the 
nucleus pulposus may herniate. Conversely, internal disc disruption

Potential Pain Generators of the Back
A useful classification system to understand the potential sources of low back 
pain depends on knowing what structures are innervated (and can transmit pain) 
and what structures have no innervation.
Innervated Structures
• Bone: vertebrae
• Joints: zygapophyseal
• Disc: only the external annulus and potentially diseased disc
• Ligaments: anterior longitudinal ligament,  posterior longitudinal ligament, 
interspinous
• Muscles and fascia
• Nerve root
Noninnervated Structures
• Ligamentum flavum
• Disc: internal annulus, nucleus pulposus

can occur without herniation because age and repeated stresses act-
ing on the spine cause the gelatinous nucleus to become more  
fibrous over time. T ears in the annulus can eventually progress to 
tears in the fibrous disc material, resulting in internal disc disrup-
tion without frank herniation. These processes result in a loss of disc 
height as well as lateral recess narrowing, foraminal narrowing, and 
potential nerve root impingement.
The loss of disc height also places stress on the posterior ele -
ments, resulting in instability of the zygapophyseal joints and 
further degeneration. The degenerative changes that occur in the 
zygapophyseal joints from aging and repetitive microtrauma are 
similar to those that occur in the appendicular skeletal joints. The 
process begins with synovial hypertrophy, which eventually results 
in cartilage degeneration and destruction. With the resultant cap-
sular laxity, the joint can become unstable, and with subsequent 
repetitive abnormal joint motion, bony hypertrophy results, thus 
narrowing the central canal and lateral recesses as well as poten -
tially impinging the nerve roots.
These changes are commonly described as segmental dysfunc -
tion. Segmental dysfunction can occur when either a segment is 
too stiff or too mobile. A segment encompasses the disc, the ver -
tebrae on each side of the disc, and the muscles and ligaments that 
act across this area. Excessive mobility, also called instability, or 
potentially better termed functional instability, can be the result of 
tissue damage, poor muscle endurance, or poor muscular control 
and is usually a combination of all three. Structural changes from 
tissue damage, such as joint laxity, vertebral end-plate fractures, 
and loss of disc height, can lead to segmental dysfunction because 
of the altered anatomy.
Muscles also provide a crucial component of spinal stability and 
are one target for potential intervention through exercise. Under 
normal circumstances, only a small amount of muscular coactiva-
tion ( 10% of maximal contraction) is needed to provide seg -
mental stability. In a segment damaged by ligamentous laxity or 
disc disease, slightly more muscle coactivation might be needed. 
Because of the relatively gentle forces required to perform activities 
of daily living, muscular endurance is more important than abso-
lute muscle strength for most patients. However, some strength 
reserve is needed for unpredictable activities such as a fall, a sudden 
load to the spine, or quick movements. In sports and heavy physi-
cal work, both strength and endurance needs increase.
This biomechanical model is particularly complex in the spine 
because of the presence of both global and segmental movement 
patterns. T wo interrelated muscular tasks must be carried out at 
the same time: (1) maintaining overall posture and position of the 
spine and (2) controlling individual intersegmental relationships. 
Sufficient but not excessive joint stiffness is required at the seg -
mental level to prevent injury and allow for efficient movement. 
This stiffness is achieved with specific patterns of muscle activity, 
which differ depending on the position of the joint and the load 
on the spine. The inability to achieve this stiffness and the result-
ing segmental problems are thought to be factors in LBP .283 Alter-
natively, some segments are thought to be too stiff because of  
osteoarthritis and ligamentous thickening in the spine, which also 
may be a source of back pain.
Although this theory offers an explanation as to how the spine 
ages, it is unclear why there is such a pronounced disconnect be -
tween the occurrence of back pain and the anatomic changes in 
the spine associated with aging. Many patients with normal spine 
anatomy suffer from back pain, while others with marked degen -
erative changes on their imaging are pain free. One theory regard-
ing the limited correlation between the severity of anatomic 
changes on imaging and the intensity of back pain expressed is the 
potential difference in muscular activation and neural control of 
segments rather than the anatomic changes themselves.
There appear to be consistent muscular problems in patients 
with chronic LBP . Some of these factors may exist preinjury, mak-
ing the spine more susceptible to damage, and some are adapta -
tions to pain. Motor systems and their adaptation to back pain 
appear to vary greatly between individuals, ranging from subtle 
changes in muscular activation (to help redistribute forces on the 
spine) to complete avoidance of activity. Studies have shown  
abnormal firing patterns in the deep stabilizers of the spine and 
transversus abdominis with activities such as limb movements, 
accepting a heavy load, and responding to balance challenges. 
Other researchers have found strength ratio abnormalities and 
endurance deficits in patients with LBP , such as abnormal flexion-
extension strength ratios and lack of endurance of torso muscles.232
These motor adaptations may have lasting long-term conse -
quences.148 Studies have demonstrated age-related changes in the 
motor cortex that affect the recruitment of spinal stabilizers, 
showing differences between younger and older individuals. 322 
Research studies on the lumbar paraspinal muscles have identified 
several abnormalities in patients with LBP . Specifically, imaging 
studies often demonstrate paraspinal muscle atrophy, particularly 
of the multifidi, in those with chronic LBP .283 Notably, recovery 
of the multifidi does not seem to occur spontaneously with the 
resolution of back pain. 147 Biopsies of the multifidi in LBP  
patients, including those collected during surgery for disc hernia-
tion, have shown abnormalities such as type 2 muscle fiber atro -
phy and structural changes in type 1 fibers. Even 5 years later, 
repeat biopsies have found type 2 fiber atrophy in patients who 
improved with surgery as well as those who did not. However, in 
the group that experienced improvement, there was a reduction in 
the percentage of abnormal type 1 fibers, whereas in the group 
with negative outcomes there was a significant increase in abnor -
mal type 1 fibers.277
Centralization and Pain
The theory of pain centralization offers an explanation for the 
discrepancy often observed between imaging findings and a  
patient’s back pain symptoms. Nociception, or the perception of 
pain, is processed by the body through a complex network of 
pathways that is far beyond the simplistic notion of a direct loop 
from injury to pain perception. Pain processing begins in the 
spinal cord and is extensively modulated in the brain, with the 
final pain experience being the result of multiple interacting  
facilitatory and inhibitory pathways. There is substantial evidence 
supporting the idea that persistent pain may be due to central 
sensitization, a condition in which the nervous system becomes 
hypersensitive. This theory could help explain why a specific pain 
generator is often not identified in cases of chronic LBP .73
Psychosocial Factors and Low Back Pain
Pain is an individual experience, and biomechanical and neuro -
logic factors alone do not explain all the variance seen clinically in 
patients with back pain. Multiple psychosocial factors have been 
found to play a role in the clinical presentation of those with LBP . 
Among the primary challenges in the evaluation of LBP is under-
standing the relative contribution of these factors, including 
anxiety, fear, suffering, and other emotional responses that con -
tribute to a patient’s distress and functional disability. These issues

may be more problematic than any contribution of nociception; 
this topic is briefly discussed here and more thoroughly discussed 
in the chapter on chronic pain because these issues are shared by 
multiple painful conditions.
Depression, Anxiety, and Anger
It appears that between 30% and 40% of those with chronic back 
pain also have depression. 204,358 This includes patients with pre -
morbid depression who develop back pain and those with back 
pain who subsequently develop depression. Patients who suffer 
from depression are at increased risk of developing back and neck 
pain. In an analysis of factors leading to the onset of back and 
neck pain, those in the highest quartile for depression scores had 
a fourfold risk of developing LBP compared to those in the lowest 
quartile.44 Further evidence also suggests that psychosocial factors 
are closely linked to the transition from acute to chronic pain and 
disability. In a study of 1628 patients with back pain seen in a 
pain clinic, those with a comorbid diagnosis of depression were 
three times more likely to be in the worst quartiles of physical and 
emotional functioning on the Short-Form 36 Health Survey than 
those who were not depressed. 118 Multiple other studies have 
found that depression, anxiety, and distress are strongly related to 
pain intensity, duration, and disability.209
Depression, anxiety, posttraumatic stress disorder, and sub -
stance use disorders are all associated with chronic back pain. 358 
In general, this relationship is bidirectional, meaning that those 
with the aforementioned mental health disorders are at increased 
risk of chronic pain and that these conditions arise in the setting 
of chronic pain. 320 Research has also shown a high correlation 
with anger measurements and pain, which is thought to be related 
to deficient opioid modulation in those with high anxiety, anger, 
and fear reactivity.35
Patient Beliefs and Fear Avoidance
Beliefs about back pain are highly individualized and vary widely 
among patients and providers. Even so, beliefs that negative con -
sequences from LBP are largely unavoidable are common across 
cultures.242 Negative belief structures are often associated with 
older age, poor self-rated general and mental health, and lower 
levels of education and income. 242 These beliefs can potentially 
hinder patients’ response to treatment and negatively interact 
with the mental health challenges associated with chronic pain.
A particularly important belief structure in those with LBP is 
fear avoidance. This term refers to the belief that movement or 
activity will be associated with further injury. In its more  
extreme form, fear avoidance manifests as catastrophizing, where 
the fears are more exaggerated. 379 Studies have demonstrated 
that patients with chronic LBP who perform poorly on tread -
mill exercise tests, 303 walk slower on treadmill tests, 6 and per -
form more poorly on spinal isometric exercise testing were the 
ones with more anticipation of pain than those who did well on 
these tests.7 Fear avoidance beliefs rather than actual pain during 
testing predicted their performance. Similarly, fear avoidance 
and catastrophizing are more predictive of pain-related disability 
than the level of pain. 379
Fear avoidance levels have been shown to predict self-reported 
disability and time off from work more accurately than actual 
pain levels or medical diagnosis.218 This has led Waddell and oth-
ers to conclude that “the fear of pain may be more disabling than 
pain itself,” which links fear avoidance beliefs to poor outcomes 
in patients with LBP . A systematic review by Wertli et al. found 
that higher levels of fear avoidance are particularly predictive in 
those with subacute LBP .377 In cases of chronic LBP , however, 
strong fear avoidance beliefs about work are associated with ongo-
ing sick leave even after 1 year.350
T o this end, Steiger et al. investigated the relationship between 
physiologic and clinical responses to exercise and found only a 
weak correlation, suggesting that exercise offers additional bene -
fits beyond just physical improvements. 334 They proposed that 
focusing on enhancing self-control and reducing fear-avoidant 
beliefs could be an effective therapeutic approach. This idea is 
central to treatment strategies such as the Start Back screening 
tool, where higher risk patients receive psychologically informed 
physical therapy.145 Additionally, multidisciplinary pain programs 
have been successful in reducing fear-avoidant beliefs and cata -
strophizing.328
Recognizing the coexistence or risk of developing mental 
health disorders and maladaptive belief systems in patients with 
LBP is crucial because these factors significantly influence treat -
ment outcomes. While much focus is often placed on identifying 
potential pain generators within the spine, the impact of psycho -
logical factors on clinical presentation underscores the need to 
approach chronic back pain through a biopsychosocial lens. View-
ing LBP solely as an anatomic issue governed by nociceptive 
pathways fails to align with current evidence. Effective treatment 
should therefore encompass functional goals, address negative 
belief structures, and enhance self-efficacy.
History and Physical Examination of  
the Low Back
A thorough history and physical examination are essential in 
evaluating LBP . They help to identify possible causes, rule out 
serious medical conditions, and determine the need for further 
diagnostic testing. This process must consider the psychological 
factors discussed earlier, along with a firm understanding of 
anatomy, pain referral patterns, systemic diseases, and the poten -
tial for nonspinal sources contributing to the patient’s symptoms. 
It is also important to acknowledge the limitations of what are 
considered objective findings, especially regarding pain, which is 
a subjective experience. Ultimately, the goals of the history and 
physical examination are to identify any underlying pathology 
and to explain the patient’s pain and disability through a compre-
hensive assessment that includes medical history, psychosocial 
evaluation, and physical exam findings.
History
As with any pain history, features of back pain that should be 
explored include location, character, severity, timing (including 
onset, duration, and frequency), alleviating and aggravating fac -
tors, and associated signs and symptoms. These details are  
intended to help clinicians create a differential diagnosis, perform 
diagnostic testing, and choose treatment options. Elements of 
history that suggest the presence of a serious underlying condi -
tion, such as cancer, infection, tumor, significant neurologic  
injury, and fracture, are called red flags (Box 34.3). Unfortunately, 
there are limited data on the actual clinical utility of many of these 
red flags, and it has been estimated that up to 80% of patients in 
a primary care practice may endorse at least one red flag. 85,359 In 
addition, a recent study found that 64% of patients with a spinal

malignancy had no red flags. 273 Unfortunately, the presence or 
absence of red flag symptoms is not proof of disease and should 
be viewed as a way to triage the risk profile. Interpreting red flags 
in the clinical context of a patient’s presentation is essential, main-
taining the fundamental goal of not missing significant spinal 
pathology.
Beyond the goal of determining a diagnosis, one rationale for 
obtaining a history is to explore the patient’s perspective and 
illness experience. Certain psychosocial factors are valuable in 
determining prognosis (e.g., poor job satisfaction, catastrophic 
thinking, the presence of depression, and excessive rest or 
downtime), which are much more common in patients in 
whom back pain becomes disabling. These factors are called 
yellow flags because the clinician should proceed cautiously and 
understand that the patient’s symptoms and potential response 
to treatment may be influenced by psychosocial factors  
(Box 34.4 ). It then becomes important to address these factors 
in treatment and to more fully explore the patient’s goals and 
barriers to improvement.
Some of these psychosocial factors are addressed by specific 
questions, and some become evident through statements patients 
make during history taking as they describe their illness experi -
ence. Questions about what patients believe is causing the pain, 
their fears and feelings surrounding this belief, their expectations 
about the pain and its treatment, and how the pain is affecting 
their lives (including work and home life) can provide valuable 
insight. Asking about the patient’s goals, why they feel certain 
treatments failed, their emotional state, sleep quality, and atti -
tude toward exercise may allow for a more complete understand -
ing of how to direct their care. Many of these yellow flags are 
better prognostic indicators than the more traditional medical 
diagnoses.364
Physical Examination
Table 34.1 outlines a thorough examination of the lumbar spine.
Observation
Observation should include a survey of the skin, muscle mass, 
bony structures, and overall posture ( Figs. 34.13  and 34.14;  
Table 34.2) as well as the position of the lumbar spine. Scoliosis 
and related spinal deformities can often be identified, as can evi -
dence of prior surgery. Gait should be observed for clues regarding 
etiology and contributing factors. The patient’s preferred position 
or posture and degree of spontaneous movement in the examina -
tion room may also provide helpful information. For example, 
patients with disc pathology may prefer to stand rather than sit, 
and those with a fracture may avoid weight bearing. Examiners 
can also get a sense for the patient’s mood and affect.
Palpation
Palpation should begin superficially and progress to deeper tis -
sues. It can be done with the patient standing or prone. As with 
other orthopedic tests, palpation over defined bony structures can 
be useful. One of the key determinants in evaluating LBP is the 
presence of focal tenderness vs. widespread pain with palpation. 
The latter tends to imply the presence of broader pain mecha -
nisms and potentially some component of fear. Given the sub -
stantial overlap of pain referral patterns from specific structures 
and the depth of the lumbar spine, care should be taken to avoid 
overinterpreting the presence of focal tenderness in any one place.
A significant limitation of any palpatory finding or test of mo-
tion is the generally poor interrater reliability seen in many studies 
correlating objective findings or anatomic localization to that 
noted by examiners.63,136,259 As an example, a study of five experi-
enced manipulative physical therapists found that they could  
accurately identify the specific level of a spinous process only 47% 
of the time when using radiographic confirmation. 136 Given the 
extensive data in this regard, any specific determination made via 
palpation alone should be interpreted with caution.
Range of Motion
Several methods can be used to measure spinal range of motion 
(ROM), including use of a single or double inclinometer; measur-
ing the distance of fingertips to floor; and, for forward flexion, the 
Schober test (measuring distraction between two marks on the 
skin during forward flexion). Of these methods, the double incli-
nometer has been shown to correlate the closest to measurements

on radiographs.126 Fingertip to floor has good interrater and in -
trarater reliability, but it takes into account the movement of the 
pelvis and is affected by structures outside the spine, such as tight 
hamstrings.
265 Schober test is commonly used to assess a decrease 
in forward flexion in ankylosing spondylitis; it is sensitive for this 
condition but not specific. General figures for normal ROM are 
forward flexion, 40 to 60 degrees; extension, 20 to 35 degrees; 
lateral flexion, 15 to 20 degrees; and rotation, 3 to 18 degrees. 
Studies to determine normal ROM in adults who are asymptom-
atic have found large variations.
261
The importance of decreased ROM in patients with back pain is 
unclear because many people without back pain also have limited 
range. ROM can also change depending on the time of day, the 
effort the patient expends, and many other factors.
391 Although 
quantifying ROM has significantly limited clinical utility, there is 
some benefit to understanding a patient’s voluntary or comfortable 
ROM and the motion preference in the context of overall presenta-
tion. This finding may provide insight into an individual’s confi-
dence in movement or the presence or absence of a significant  
injury. Although not particularly sensitive or specific, pain with 
flexion is often associated with disc pathology, and pain with exten-
sion is often associated with spinal canal stenosis or posterior  
element pathology. As with palpatory findings, all physical exami-
nation findings taken to be diagnostic for a particular source of pain 
tend to have low levels of interrater reliability, rendering them a 
piece of the puzzle but not particularly useful in isolation.

Neurologic Examination
A neurologic examination of the lower limbs is crucial in assess -
ing patients with LBP because it helps identify significant neuro-
logic abnormalities or the underlying causes of leg pain, such as 
radiculopathy, peripheral polyneuropathy, mononeuropathy, or 
conditions affecting anterior horn cells or upper motor neurons 
(Table 34.3). The examination should be conducted in a system-
atic manner, progressing through reflex, motor, and sensory 
evaluations. Observing and documenting neurologic deficits, 
consistency, and patterns that correspond to root-level pathology 
or peripheral nerve distributions can provide valuable insights.
Additionally, evaluating for upper motor neuron signs is  
essential. Conversely, the significance of any single finding in 
isolation is generally limited; for instance, the accuracy of the 
neurologic examination in diagnosing a herniated disc is moder -
ate. However, diagnostic accuracy can be enhanced when multiple 
findings are considered in combination. The sensitivity and speci-
ficity of various findings for lumbar radiculopathy have been 
thoroughly studied (Table 34.4).
Examining the Area Above and Below the Lumbar Spine
Similar to the evaluation of other joints, the areas above and  
below the lumbar spine should be evaluated to be sure nothing is 
missed. ROM of the hip joints should always be assessed, and a 
quick screen of the knee and ankle joints may help determine 
whether these areas are contributing to the back problem. The 
thoracic spine can be quickly screened as well during ROM and 
palpation.
Musculoskeletal and Neurologic Specific Testing
After examining the low back and surrounding structures, clini -
cians often use special tests to further identify the source of 
discomfort. These diagnostic maneuvers apply functional stress 
to specific structures, helping to isolate the various contributors 
to a patient’s LBP . They are essential in identifying patterns that 
may not be apparent from patient history alone and guide the 
use of additional diagnostic tools, such as electromyography, 
radiography, computed tomography (CT), or MRI. However, 
each maneuver has limited sensitivity and specificity, and the 
results can be influenced by multiple factors. Therefore positive 
findings should be interpreted cautiously, especially when con -
sidered in isolation. Petersen et al.

express their distress. Research has shown that patients with chronic 
LBP often experience heightened anxiety during physical examina-
tions, sometimes reaching levels comparable to those seen in panic 
attacks. This anxiety is generally manifest as avoidance behavior, 
such as decreased ROM or poor effort with muscle testing.
131
Other reasons for illness behavior include fear, malingering, and 
an inherent belief in or need to demonstrate disability. A well-
described method for screening for the presence of nonorganic 
contributions to a clinical presentation are Waddell signs.
364 
Waddell et al.364 described five physical findings that present incon-
sistencies on examination that do not have clear anatomic corre -
lates. The presence of at least three of these is felt to indicate that 
there is a nonorganic contribution to the patient’s presentation. 
However, this does not indicate an absence of significant pathology 
or the presence of malingering. The five maneuvers are as follows:
• Inappropriate tenderness that is widespread or superficial
• Pain on testing that only simulates loading the spine, such 
as light pressure applied to the top of the head, which re -
produces back pain, or rotating the hips and shoulders  
together to simulate twisting without actually moving the 
spine, which reproduces back pain
• Inconsistent performance when testing the same thing in 
different positions, such as a difference in outcome of the 
straight leg–raising test with the patient supine vs. sitting
• Regional deficits in strength or sensation that do not have 
an anatomic basis
• Overreaction during the physical examination
Clinical Evaluation: Diagnostics
Imaging Studies
Imaging of the lumbar spine is typically recommended when 
there is a need to assess for significant pathology, such as red 
flag conditions, or to guide treatment decisions. Due to the 
high prevalence of abnormal findings in lumbar spine radiogra -
phy, it is important that imaging is performed with clear intent 
and that the results are interpreted within the appropriate 
clinical context. Imaging often adds another layer of informa-
tion that must be carefully considered alongside other clinical 
data to form a comprehensive understanding of the patient’s 
condition.

SECTION 4  Issues in Specific Diagnoses
Plain Radiography
Conventional radiographs are indicated after trauma to evaluate for 
fracture and to look for bony lesions such as tumor when red flags are 
present. They can also help with identifying scoliosis or segmental 
anomalies and may allow for evaluation of related structures such as the 
hips, pelvis, and sacroiliac joints. However, as an initial screening tool 
for lumbar spine pathology, they have very low sensitivity and specific-
ity.
121 Anteroposterior (AP) and lateral views are the two commonly 
obtained views. Ideally, these should be obtained standing to get a bet-
ter assessment of scoliosis, spondylolisthesis, or other spinal deformities. 
In patients with LBP , it is often helpful to obtain standing flexion/ 
extension views to more fully assess for spondylolisthesis, which is a 
relatively common finding in older adults. Oblique views can be ob-
tained to examine for a spondylolysis by visualizing the pars interarticu-
laris and the “Scottie dog” appearance of the lumbar spine (Fig. 34.15). 
Pars fractures that are seen on oblique radiographs are present in  
approximately 5% of the adult population and are largely asymptomatic, 
so care should be taken not to overinterpret their significance.
330
Magnetic Resonance Imaging
MRI is the primary imaging tool for evaluating IVDs, the spinal 
canal, vertebrae, and surrounding structures. It is especially effective

for identifying disc herniations, lumbar spinal stenosis, fractures, 
tumors, infections, and other pathologies (Fig. 34.16). The use of 
gadolinium contrast enhances MRI by highlighting structures 
with increased vascularity, making it particularly useful for assess-
ing tumors, infections, or scar tissue. In cases of scar tissue, which 
is vascular, contrast-enhanced MRI can distinguish it from avas -
cular recurrent disc herniation. This technique is especially valu-
able when evaluating patients with recurrent radicular symptoms 
following previous surgery.
The limitation of MRI is that, despite being highly sensitive, it 
is not very specific in identifying the exact source of pain. It is well 
documented that many people without back pain show degenera-
tive changes, disc bulges, and protrusions on MRI scans. Boden 
et al.
26 found that one-third of 67 asymptomatic individuals had 
a “substantial abnormality” on MRI of the lumbar spine. Among 
those under 60 years old, 20% had a disc herniation, while 36% 
of those over 60 had a disc herniation, and 21% had spinal steno-
sis. Bulging and degenerative discs were even more commonly 
observed. In another lumbar MRI study involving people without 
back pain, Jensen et al.
157 found that only 36% of the 98 patients 
had normal discs. The study revealed that disc bulges and protru-
sions were common in asymptomatic individuals, while extru -
sions were not. The 2001 Laidback study by Jarvik et al. 155  
confirmed these findings and determined that baseline MRI  
results were not strong predictors of future pain. Long-term out -
comes from subsequent analyses of the Laidback study data reveal 
that the incidence of new MRI findings over a 3-year period was 
low (2% to 8%).
342 Importantly, these new findings, including 
annular fissures, disc extrusions, and nerve root impingement, 
were not strongly associated with the development of chronic 
LBP or radicular symptoms. This suggests that baseline MRI find-
ings in asymptomatic individuals are not predictive of future LBP .
In general, clinical practice guidelines recommend against  
ordering MRI for acute, uncomplicated episodes of LBP . Jacobs  
et al. studied this population and found
152 MRI ordered within  
6 weeks of symptom onset was associated with more surgery, 
greater costs of care, greater prescription opioid use, and worse 
pain at follow-up. In fact, in the early MRI group, lumbar surgery 
was 13 times more likely when compared to those without an 
early scan (1.48% vs. 0.09%).
152 In a similar study, early lumbar 
MRI increased health care costs, health care utilization, and

Computed Tomography
Due to its resolution of soft tissue anatomy, MRI has largely re -
placed CT as the preferred imaging study for LBP and radiculopa-
thy. However, CT remains more effective than MRI for evaluating 
bony lesions. CT scans are also valuable for postoperative patients 
with surgical hardware, which can interfere with MRI images, and 
for those with implants such as aneurysm clips or pacemakers that 
make MRI unsuitable. Additionally, CT can be combined with 
myelography to enhance the visualization of the spinal canal and 
nerve roots. In myelography, contrast dye is injected into the dural 
sac, and plain radiographs are performed to produce images of the 
borders and contents of the dural sac ( Fig. 34.17). CT images 
subsequently can be obtained to produce axial cross-sectional im-
ages of the spine that enhance the distinction between the dural sac 
and its surrounding structures. This modality is particularly useful 
when there is hardware present that degrades MRI images or in 
those who have contraindications to MRI.
Scintigraphy
Radionuclear bone scanning is a fairly sensitive but not specific 
imaging modality that can be used to detect occult fractures, 
bony metastases, and infections (Fig. 34.18). T o increase ana -
tomic specificity, single-photon emission computed tomography 
(SPECT) bone scanning is used to obtain bone scans with axial 
slices, which allows the diagnostician to differentiate uptake in 
the posterior elements from more anterior structures of the spine. 
The diagnostic use of this study with regard to altering clinical 
decision making is controversial. Studies have been published 
demonstrating that the use of SPECT can help to identify pa-
tients with LBP who might benefit from zygapophyseal joint 
injections
268; however, the literature is conflicting. Freiermuth 
et al. reported SPECT/CT scans showed only moderate sensitivity 
and specificity and did not recommend this test as a first-line 
diagnostic tool prior to diagnostic infiltrations.
107 It also can be 
particularly helpful in the identification of a painful pars lesion 
in adolescent athletes.
330
Electromyography
Electromyography is useful in the evaluation of patients with 
neurologic symptoms or findings because it provides a physiologic 
measure for detecting neurogenic changes and denervation with 
good sensitivity and high specificity. It can help provide informa-
tion as to which anatomic lesions found on imaging studies  
are truly physiologically significant.
288 It can also help in the dis -
tinction between radiculopathy, peripheral mononeuropathy,  
peripheral polyneuropathy, and other lower motor neuron

has limited specificity for radiculopathy and that its sensitivity 
also decreases with time. It also does not assess for the presence  
or absence of radicular pain or isolated nerve root sensory  
dysfunction.
Laboratory Studies
Laboratory tests are not routinely used in diagnosing LBP; how -
ever, they may play an important role in identifying serious un-
derlying conditions when certain red flag signs are present. These 
include a history of trauma, cancer, unexplained weight loss,  
immunosuppression, illicit drug use, and prolonged use of corti -
costeroids or biologics. In addition, certain rheumatologic condi-
tions and infections may warrant further laboratory tests, such as 
erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein 
(CRP), whereas certain neoplasms, such as multiple myeloma, 
may require serum and protein electrophoresis.
Treatment: Back Pain Greater Than Leg Pain
Nonspecific Low Back Pain
Nearly 85% of those who seek medical care for LBP do not  
receive a specific diagnosis.80 Most of these patients have a multi-
factorial source for their back pain, which may include factors such  
as deconditioning, poor muscle activation, emotional stress, and 
age or injury-related changes such as disc degeneration, arthritis, 
and ligament hypertrophy. This type of back pain is referred to by 
**

various terms, including nonspecific LBP , simple backache,  
mechanical LBP , lumbar strain, dorsalgia, and spinal degenera -
tion. By definition, the patient’s history and physical examination 
do not point to a more specific diagnosis, and diagnostic tests 
used to rule out other possible causes come back negative.  
Research has shown that factors such as obesity, smoking, a sed -
entary lifestyle, vigorous physical activity (representing both ends 
of the activity spectrum), and genetic predisposition are signifi -
cant risk factors for nonspecific LBP . Interestingly, most abnor-
malities detected on MRI, as well as work-related activities such 
as lifting, twisting, standing, and assuming awkward positions, 
have not been identified as risk factors.
15 T reatment options for 
nonspecific LBP will be discussed in a later section.
Lumbar Spondylosis
The term lumbar spondylosis is often used instead of nonspecific 
LBP to achieve a more precise diagnosis and to identify subgroups 
of patients, particularly older individuals, who may benefit from 
specific treatments. Degenerative changes in the facet joints  
frequently coexist with degenerative disc disease and other age-  
related spinal conditions, making it challenging to distinguish be-
tween these entities. Studies by Mooney and Robertson,
241 as well 
as McCall et al.,229 have examined the sclerotomal distribution of 
zygapophyseal joint pain in detail. This type of pain typically radi-
ates to the buttock and, in some cases, can extend below the knee.
Delineating a degenerative facet joint as the primary pain gen-
erator in axial LBP is difficult. Imaging studies are not particularly 
useful because many people who are asymptomatic also have radio-
graphic spondylosis. There is no association between radiologic os-
teoarthritis of the facet joints and presence of LBP ,
213 nor is there a 
unique identifying feature found in the history, physical examina-
tion, or radiologic imaging that is diagnostic for zygapophyseal 
joint pain.
213 The only diagnostic maneuvers for zygapophyseal 
joint pain are fluoroscopically guided zygapophyseal joint injections 
with local anesthetic and medial branch blocks (i.e., local anesthetic 
blocks of the medial branches of the dorsal primary rami that  
innervate the zygapophyseal joints).
86,227 With these injection tech-
niques, the prevalence of facet-mediated pain in chronic LBP suf-
ferers has been estimated to be 15% in the younger population and 
40% in older age groups.
307,310,312 In 1994, Schwarzer et al. 307 
demonstrated that the vast majority of lumbar facet joint pain 
originates from the L4-L5 and L5-S1 facet joints. Noninvasive 
treatments are similar to treatments for osteoarthritic joints and can 
be categorized as lifestyle modification, activity modification, med-
ication, and exercise; they are described in the section on treatment.
Lumbar Disc Disease
Identifying discogenic causes of LBP is an effort to distinguish 
these patients from the broader group with nonspecific LBP . 
Discogenic pain can arise from three main categories: degenera -
tive disc disease, internal disc disruption, and disc herniation. 
While discogenic pain is traditionally described as a bandlike 
pain worsened by lumbar flexion, it can also present as unilateral 
pain, radiate to the buttock, and even worsen with extension or 
side bending, depending on the location of the disc pathology. 
Diagnosing discogenic LBP is challenging because disc degenera -
tion often appears on imaging in asymptomatic individu-
als.
26,155,157 Discography, a controversial diagnostic tool, is  
typically used as a presurgical screening method for identifying 
discogenic pain.
337

Internal Disc Disruption
Internal disc disruption is a condition in which the internal archi-
tecture of the disc is disrupted, but its external surface remains 
essentially normal (i.e., there is no bulge or herniation). It is char-
acterized by degradation of the nucleus pulposus and radial fissures 
that extend to the outer third of the annulus (high-intensity zone 
areas on MRI).
11 Diagnosis requires reproduction of pain on dis -
cography and annular fissure on postdiscography CT .28 Although 
the use of discography is controversial, most believe that annular 
tears (especially those that reach the outer third of the annulus  
[i.e., the innervated fibers]) can be a source of LBP . However, an-
nular tears or high-intensity zones can be seen on MRIs of patients 
that are asymptomatic. The proposed mechanisms for pain genera-
tion from internal disc disruption are chemical nociception from 
inflammatory mediators and mechanical stimulation.
34,254
Disc Herniation
The terminology used to describe disc material that extends be -
yond the IVD space is often applied incorrectly. Common  
descriptors for this condition include herniated disc, herniated 
nucleus pulposus, disc bulge, disc protrusion, ruptured disc, and 
prolapsed disc, which are often used interchangeably. When disc 
material is displaced greater than 25% of its circumference, it is 
referred to as a disc bulge. Conversely, a disc herniation is more 
focal, involves less than 25% of its circumference ( Fig. 34.19), 
and is classified into protrusions or extrusions. A disc protrusion 
is defined as a herniation with the distance of the edges of the 
herniated material less than the distance of the edges at its base. A 
disc extrusion occurs when the distance of the edges of the herni-
ated material is greater than the distance of the edges at its base. 
A disc extrusion can be further subclassified as sequestrated if the 
extruded disc material has no continuity with the disc of origin. 
Disc herniations can also be described as contained or uncon -
tained depending on the integrity of the outer annular fibers. If 
the outer annular fibers are still intact, it is described as a con -
tained disc herniation. This classification has no relevance to the 
integrity of the posterior longitudinal ligament.
More than 95% of lumbar disc herniations occur at the L4-L5 
and L5-S1 levels
77,327; the next most common is L3-L4, followed

by L2-L3. The most common lumbosacral radiculopathies are 
consequently L5 and S1. Posterolateral disc herniations are most 
common because the annulus fibrosus is weakest at that loca-
tion.
144 Posterolateral discs can affect the nerve root as it descends 
in the lateral recess or just before it enters the neural foramen. Far 
lateral or extraforaminal herniations can affect the nerve root as it 
exits the neural foramen, and central disc herniations can affect 
any part of the cauda equina, depending on the level.
94
Disc herniations can impact the nerve root through either an 
inflammatory response, mechanical compression, or a combina -
tion of both, often resulting in radicular pain. However, disc 
herniations can also cause purely axial pain. The primary  
approach to managing discogenic back pain is conservative treat -
ment, which is generally effective for most patients. Despite this, 
some patients do not respond to conservative measures. In recent 
years, various interventional procedures have been developed to 
address discogenic back pain, aiming to avoid the need for sur -
gery. Growing evidence supports the use of epidural steroid injec-
tions (ESIs) for managing disc herniations with radiculitis, though 
they are less effective for back pain alone. Please refer to the pro -
cedures section for more information regarding ESIs.
Numerous percutaneous disc procedures are available, with 
new techniques frequently emerging to treat discogenic pain 
unresponsive to conservative treatment. However, none have 
shown superior results compared to microdiscectomy. The litera-
ture on surgical management mirrors that of ESIs: Surgery is 
most effective for alleviating radicular leg symptoms and less so 
for axial back pain. Discectomy remains the most common surgi-
cal procedure, though spinal fusion may be considered in cases of 
significant multilevel degenerative disc disease with concerns of 
instability. Presently, prosthetic disc replacements have been ex -
plored as an alternative to spinal fusion, with studies indicating 
that disc arthroplasty reduces operating time, length of hospital 
stay, and reoperation rates, but may not have better outcomes for 
pain relief.
390
Vertebral End Plates (Modic Changes)
Vertebral end-plate abnormalities seen on MRI are classified by 
different signal intensities into Modic type 1, 2, or 3. These abnor-
malities are likely a proinflammatory response to end-plate or disc 
damage; Modic type 1 changes, and perhaps other Modic changes, 
appear to correlate somewhat with the presence of LBP .
138
Prognosis of Low Back Pain
Determining the prognosis for LBP is challenging for several 
reasons. First, LBP is a symptom that can result from a wide 
range of underlying conditions, each with different outcomes. 
Second, the experience of pain is highly individual, and patients’ 
treatment expectations can vary. Research shows that pain and 
recovery outcomes are influenced by complex factors, including 
cultural, psychological, social, and economic elements.
209,264 The 
commonly cited belief that 90% of acute LBP cases resolve 
within 6 weeks does not fully capture the reality seen in clinical 
practice or reflected in the scientific literature.
Most studies focus on individuals who seek medical care  
for LBP , which may not represent the broader population of 
people who experience back pain. A meta-analysis of patients  
with less than 3 weeks of back pain, including both those receiv-
ing treatment and those in placebo groups, evaluated both the 
natural progression and the clinical course of the condition.
76,264

SECTION 4  Issues in Specific Diagnoses
The study found that most patients showed rapid improvement 
within 1 month, with pain continuing to decrease, though at a 
slower rate, until about 3 months. Beyond this period, little 
change in pain was observed up to 1 year. The risk of recurrence 
within 3 months ranged from 19% to 34%, while the risk of re -
currence within 1 year ranged from 66% to 84%. Although this 
group was diverse, most patients were diagnosed with nonspecific 
LBP . The remainder of the chapter will focus on cases of back pain 
with more specific diagnoses.
Spinal Fractures
Spinal fractures can result from various causes, including trauma, 
tumors, pathologic fractures, osteoporosis-related fractures, and 
stress or overuse injuries. These conditions are commonly seen in 
a comprehensive spine practice and are considered red flag diag -
noses, as previously discussed. A 10-year study of nearly 1500 
patients at a level 1 trauma center in Norway revealed that most 
fractures occurred in the thoracic spine. Of the patients, 5% were 
18 years or younger, while 64% were between 19 and 64 years 
old. The most common causes were falls and traffic accidents.  
Additionally, 8.5% of the fractures were associated with spinal 
cord injuries, and 16% of those admitted required surgery.
76  
Clinicians evaluating LBP should be mindful of different fracture 
types and specific conditions related to these injuries.
Spondylolysis
Spondylolysis is a defect in the pars interarticularis, the narrow 
segment of bone connecting the superior and inferior articular 
facets. Spondylolisthesis, a related condition, refers to the anterior 
displacement of one vertebra compared to the one below it, often 
described as an anterior slip. While spondylolisthesis can result 
from various causes, most commonly degenerative, isthmic spon-
dylolisthesis specifically refers to cases where fractures in the pars 
(spondylolysis) lead to an associated slippage of the VB and the 
anterior portion of the neural arch (Fig. 34.20).
L5
S1
• Fig. 34.20 Grade 1 isthmic spondylolisthesis.
Most pars fractures occur early in childhood and are typically 
asymptomatic. A landmark study by Fredrickson et al. in the 
1950s involved radiographs of 500 first-grade students, with fol -
low-up data published 45 years later. They found that 4.4% of the 
students had pars fractures at age 6 years, increasing to 6% by age 
18 years. This study reflects the baseline prevalence of pars frac-
tures observed in radiographic and population studies of adults.
106 
In large screening studies of adolescent athletes, this prevalence 
ranges from 8% to 15%.
292,325
As a clinical problem, acute isthmic spondylolysis is most typi-
cally seen in adolescent athletes. These patients present with either 
acute or gradually progressive LBP , typically without radiation into 
the lower extremities. In adolescent athletes with LBP related to 
sports activity, spondylolysis is the most commonly identified 
cause. In one adolescent sports medicine clinic, 47% of athletes 
seen for LBP were diagnosed with spondylolysis.
237 In this setting, 
pars fractures are most typically viewed as a stress injury to the 
bone related to repeated forces on the spine. Acute fracture from a 
severe hyperextension injury is also possible but less commonly 
reported.
97 Although most injuries are typically discussed in the 
setting of athletes performing frequent extension and rotation, 
such as gymnasts and football linemen, pars fractures are seen in a 
wide range of sports, and prevalence is likely related to the num-
bers of youth involved in particular sports.
96,151,313
Patients with LBP often notice that their symptoms worsen 
with activity and improve with rest. This pain can be triggered by 
spinal flexion or extension of the lumbar spine. Although the 
physical examination may reveal some focal tenderness, it is often 
otherwise unremarkable, and the neurologic exam is usually nor -
mal. However, if there are clear radicular symptoms or neurologic 
findings, there may be concern for conditions, such as spondylo -
listhesis or other causes of nerve root compression, that are not 
typically associated with an isolated pars lesion. Given the high 
prevalence of spondylolysis in adolescent athletes with LBP , clini-
cians should maintain a high level of suspicion in any young 
athlete presenting with LBP regardless of whether the physical 
examination yields positive or negative findings.
The ideal radiographic study for a suspected pars injury has 
yet to be answered. Plain radiographs are of limited use in diag -
nosing asymptomatic spondylolysis. AP and lateral radiographs, 
along with a spot view of the lumbosacral spine, should be ob -
tained. Although pars defects have traditionally been identified 
using oblique views, these views do not significantly improve the 
detection sensitivity on x-rays. Additionally, oblique x-rays in -
volve much higher radiation exposure.
139,276,329,345 AP and lateral 
standing radiographs are typically used initially to identify spon -
dylolisthesis or any major bone abnormalities. 329 SPECT scans 
are more sensitive than plain x-rays or standard bone scans, and 
a positive SPECT or bone scan often indicates a painful pars 
lesion.
139
However, retrospective analyses show that increased uptake 
can persist for up to 1 year after the fracture occurs.276 If a SPECT 
scan suggests an active pars injury, a thin-slice CT scan at the 
abnormal level can be useful to confirm the diagnosis and assess 
the stage of the lesion. Staging the lesion according to chronicity 
is helpful to predict healing.
The T okushima classification categorizes defects on CT as 
acute, progressive, or terminal ( Box 34.5).
113 In a meta-analysis 
using this classification system, 68% of acute defects healed, 28% 
of progressive lesions healed, and no terminal lesions healed.
183 
Determining chronicity can help the clinician decide how quickly 
the athlete should progress through treatment. The use of MRI in

diagnosing spondylolysis has garnered much attention in recent 
years. Standard magnetic resonance sequences identify only 80% 
of pars lesions seen on SPECT and should be used to look for 
other pathology.
228 However, Campbell et al.40 showed that non-
standard magnetic resonance sequences (oblique sagittal images) 
found 39 of 40 pars defects seen on CT and SPECT , but only CT 
correctly staged the lesion.
40 Despite this, many clinicians use 
MRI as the initial imaging modality after plain films. Some  
authors recommend MRI followed by SPECT if the MRI is 
negative and symptoms persist.
49
Successful management typically involves relative rest or cessa-
tion of physical activity that stresses the spine. Patients are  
typically advised to rest for 3 months, 329 which is the minimum 
duration during which healing of a pars lesion has been observed 
on serial imaging.
329,383 In a cohort of athletes treated for spondy-
lolysis, those athletes who rested from sport for at least 12 weeks 
were 16.4 times more likely to have the most favorable outcome 
compared with athletes who returned early.
89
Bracing, which is commonly used, has not been shown to be 
more helpful than relative rest without bracing. A meta-analysis 
demonstrated that 84% of 665 patients with spondylolysis treated 
nonoperatively were able to return to pain-free unrestricted activ-
ity by 1 year. No statistically significant difference in clinical 
outcomes was seen between the braced and nonbraced groups.
183 
A rigid brace might be considered after 2 weeks of rest if symp -
toms are not resolving.329 A radiographically stable bony union is 
the obvious goal but is not an absolute necessity given that many 
young athletes can become symptom free in sports and everyday 
life even with a persistent pars defect. That same meta-analysis 
studied 847 defects and showed a 28% radiographic healing rate; 
71% of the unilateral defects healed, and 18% of the bilateral  
defects healed.
183
Regardless of whether the young athlete is braced, there is a 
risk of deconditioning. When the pain subsides enough, the pa -
tient should be encouraged to start aerobic conditioning and 
eventually participate in a spine rehabilitation program before 
returning to sports. After mastering a basic core stabilization pro-
gram, the athlete can gradually progress back to their specific 
sport, emphasizing neuromuscular proprioceptive control and 
sport-specific drills before fully returning to play. Surgical treat -
ment is rarely indicated for the patient with spondylolysis alone 
but is more common in the setting of spondylolisthesis and/or 
radiculopathy.
The natural course of isthmic spondylolysis and low-grade 
spondylolisthesis (less than grade 2) is generally benign, with 
significant progression of the slip being rare.
25 Saraste299 demon-
strated this usual course in a study of 225 patients over a 20-year 
follow-up period. Most cases of progressive slippage occur during 
the adolescent growth spurt. Very young athletes should be 
monitored with lateral flexion-extension plain films every 6 to  
12 months until skeletal maturity is reached. Besides the adoles-
cent growth spurt, a listhesis greater than 50% is considered a risk 
factor for progressive slip.
Spondylolisthesis
Although it can occur in the setting of bilateral spondylolysis, 
lumbar spondylolisthesis needs to be considered separately from 
an acute fracture/spondylolysis itself. Spondylolisthesis is gener-
ally grouped into five different categories by etiology, excluding 
iatrogenic causes.
195,270,378 Isthmic spondylolisthesis, described 
earlier, is associated with bilateral pars fractures and commonly 
occurs at L5-S1. It is the most common type and is further sub -
divided into pars fatigue fracture, pars elongation due to multiple 
healed stress fractures, and pars acute fracture. Dysplastic spondy-
lolisthesis is caused by dysplasia of the posterior elements, leading 
to an inability to resist shear stresses and forward slippage. Degen-
erative spondylolisthesis is seen in the older spine, is related to 
progressive segmental degenerative changes, particularly in the 
facet joints, and most commonly occurs at the L4-L5 level. T rau-
matic spondylolisthesis is rare and is caused by acute fracture 
secondary to trauma. Pathologic spondylolisthesis is attributable 
to medical causes of generalized or local bone disease that can 
cause decreased bony strength. For all causes, the degree of slip is 
graded 1 through 5, ideally through the use of standing lateral 
radiographs with flexion-extension views (Table 34.6). Iatrogenic 
spondylolisthesis is related to removal of bone during laminec -
tomy without appropriate hardware placement for stabilization.
Nonoperative treatment, monitoring, and surgical care  
depend on multiple factors, including the type of slip, the age of 
the patient, the relative stability of the slip, and the presence of 
neurologic involvement. Fusion surgery is generally considered 
for isthmic spondylolisthesis in the adolescent patient if there is 
significant progression or the slip is grade 3 or greater. For degen-
erative spondylolisthesis, nonoperative management with a reha -
bilitation program similar to that described for nonspecific LBP 
is appropriate. Because a degenerative slip is often associated with 
spinal canal stenosis, operative intervention with decompression 
and possibly fusion is generally considered where there is evi -
dence of neurologic loss, progressive instability, or recalcitrant 
radicular pain.
Differential Diagnosis and Treatment:  
Leg Pain Greater Than Back Pain
The differential diagnosis for patients with leg pain greater than 
back pain is shorter than for patients with only back pain. Com -
mon causes are discussed in the following sections, as are common 
mimickers of radicular pain.
