SECTION 4  Issues in Specific Diagnoses
Lumbosacral Radiculopathy
Radicular symptoms can be the result of overt mechanical com -
pression of a nerve root or a chemically mediated inflammatory 
process. Many patients with radicular pain have no neural  
impingement noted on MRI. The primary cause of lumbar ra -
dicular pain is attributed to inflammation of the affected nerves, 
which is often due to nearby disc herniation.244 The inflammatory 
response is located at the ventral epidural space at the interface of 
disc herniation and the traversing or exiting nerve root. A herni -
ated nucleus pulposus results in inflammation in the dura, nerve 
roots, and spinal cord with increased levels of phospholipase A2 
activity.297 Extracts of disc herniations have 20 to 10,000 times 
more phospholipase A2 activity than any other human tissue. In 
addition, inflammatory markers such as matrix metalloproteinase 
activity, nitric oxide, prostaglandin E2, and interleukin-6 are also 
found.116,166,297
The clinical features of lumbar radicular pain were first out -
lined in studies of patients undergoing lumbar surgery for disc 
herniation.251,324 In one study, nerve roots were squeezed with 
forceps in awake patients during surgery. 251 In a second study, 
sutures were placed around the affected (inflamed) nerve root and 
an adjacent healthy nerve root and led out through the surgical 
incision to the skin and left in place. 324 The sutures were then 
pulled on the following day. Squeezing or pulling on normal 
nerve roots did not elicit lumbar radicular pain. Only if the nerve 
root was previously inflamed did mechanical stimulation evoke 
radicular pain. The radicular pain produced from these studies 
was described as lancinating, traveling down the length of the 
lower extremity in a distinct, narrow band. The pain referral pat -
tern was similar between L4, L5, and S1.32,251,324 For compression 
alone to be painful, the dorsal root ganglion (DRG) must be  
involved, which has been demonstrated in animal models but not 
in human studies.150
A neurophysiologic correlation of lumbar radicular pain has 
been provided by animal studies. 32,150 In laboratory animals, 
squeezing normal nerve roots evokes only momentary discharge 
of A-delta and C fibers. Compressing the DRG triggers tempo -
rary activation in all nerve fibers (A-beta, A-delta, and C fibers), 
but sustained activity in all afferent fibers is only observed when 
the nerve root has been previously injured or inflamed. These 
findings suggest that radicular pain is not due solely to discharges 
from nociceptive afferents (C fibers) but a heterospecific discharge 
of all sensory afferent fibers (A-beta, A-delta, and C fibers). The 
quality of radicular pain is often described as lancinating, shock -
like, and electric, which is evidence of more than nociceptive  
afferent fibers firing. Furthermore, the distribution of radicular 
pain is not dermatomal in distribution. If anything, the distribu -
tion of radicular pain corresponds to the sclerotome of the spinal 
level involved, which has previously been demonstrated in the 
cervical spine and more recently in the lumbar spine. 115
The most frequent cause of radiculopathy is disc protrusion. In 
fewer than 1% of patients presenting with radicular symptoms, 
other causes (infection, malignancy, fracture) are identified. 74 
However, in cases of radiculopathy accompanied by unusual 
symptoms, such as fever, weight loss, night pain, a history of can-
cer, or risk factors for osteoporosis, it is crucial to evaluate for 
these rarer but potentially more serious underlying conditions. 
The most common levels of disc herniation are L4-L5 and L5-S1, 
with L5 and S1 being the most common nerve roots involved  
in radiculopathy. Multiple nerve roots can be affected by a  
single disc herniation, given the organization of the cauda equina. 
The affected nerve root level might not correlate with the level of 
the disc herniation. For example, a central L3-L4 disc herniation 
could impact the L5 or S1 rootlets as they descend through the 
thecal sac before exiting out of their expected neural foramen. 
T rue cauda equina syndrome is a surgical emergency and occurs 
when the lowest sacral rootlets are affected, resulting in bowel, 
bladder, and sexual dysfunction. Up to 1% of all disc herniations 
present as cauda equina syndrome, and recovery of neurologic 
deficits, including bowel and bladder dysfunction, is greatest if 
decompressive surgery is performed within 48 hours. 140 In the 
appropriate clinical setting, a large postvoid residual of urine is a 
good predictor of cauda equina syndrome.74
The natural course of lumbosacral radiculopathy due to disc 
herniation often leads to spontaneous resolution of symptoms 
over time. Numerous studies have demonstrated that disc protru-
sions and extrusions can regress without the need for surgical  
intervention. Conservative treatment is typically the preferred ap-
proach to alleviate pain and enhance functional capacity during 
the acute phase of radiculopathy. Even in cases involving some 
degree of neurologic injury, conservative management should still 
be considered because research has shown comparable neurologic 
recovery outcomes between patients treated surgically and those 
managed nonsurgically.372
However, the specifics of conservative management for lumbo-
sacral radiculopathies are still unclear. NSAIDs are no more  
effective in reducing sciatica pain compared with placebo or other 
drugs.52,278,279,356 Most studies fail to show significant benefit 
from oral steroids for treatment of acute radiculopathy, especially 
in terms of surgical rates and pain scores; however, they may play 
a small role in temporarily improving function in the acute 
phase.51,124 Neuropathic agents such as anticonvulsants and tricy-
clic antidepressants (TCAs) are often used for radicular pain. 51 
Small studies have found gabapentin and topiramate to be associ-
ated with small improvements in pain scores. 52 Exercise therapy 
has not specifically been demonstrated to alter the course of acute 
radiculopathy but is typically recommended in guidelines. 335
Lumbar ESIs have become a common adjuvant for the treat -
ment of lumbosacral radicular pain. The literature supports use of 
fluoroscopically guided transforaminal epidural injections for 
short-term improvement in pain and possibly to reduce the need 
for surgery. ESIs are best used in combination with an active re -
habilitation program because they decrease pain and inflamma -
tion. See the section on ESIs for further details regarding the 
procedure and outcomes.
Surgical management of lumbosacral radiculopathy is best  
reserved for patients who have neurologic progression, cauda equina 
syndrome, or persistent radicular symptoms despite maximized 
conservative management for 6 to 8 weeks. Common decompressive 
procedures with favorable outcomes include lumbar hemilami -
notomy with discectomy and lumbar hemilaminectomy. 336 The 
Spine Patient Outcome Research T rial compared the effect  
of surgery over individualized nonoperative treatment across  
13 spine centers in the United States with 501 surgical candidates 
experiencing lumbar radicular symptoms of at least 6-week dura -
tion, which were caused by a disc herniation. 244,375 Individuals 
randomized to either surgery or nonoperative treatment improved 
substantially over the 2-year period of the study, and the primary 
outcomes were not statistically significantly different between the 
groups. However, a secondary outcome of sciatica severity showed 
statistical significance in favor of surgery.
Patients should be counseled regarding appropriate expecta -
tions after surgery for lumbar disc herniation with radiculopathy.

Surgery might slightly accelerate the resolution of neurologic 
deficits for the typical radiculopathy; however, the major benefit 
of surgical intervention is pain relief.
79 Relief of leg pain should 
be expected, although back pain relief is more difficult to predict. 
Patients should be counseled that they are likely to have recurrent 
back difficulties even after successful decompressive surgery.
343 
Fig. 34.21 shows a useful algorithmic approach to the manage -
ment of acute lumbosacral radiculopathy.
Lumbar Spinal Stenosis
The symptoms of spinal stenosis arise from a complex series of 
changes within the lumbar spine, which are often related to  
aging.
119 However, not all patients with significant narrowing 
experience symptoms. If mechanical compression alone were the 
cause of spinal stenosis, then decompressive surgery would be  
the definitive cure for neurogenic claudication. Because this is not 
the case, alternative theories about the pathogenesis of symptom -
atic spinal stenosis have been explored. T wo theories supporting a 
vascular cause for symptoms of spinal stenosis are the venous  
engorgement and arterial insufficiency theories.
3 In the venous 
engorgement theory, the spinal veins of patients with stenosis di -
late, causing venous congestion and stagnating blood flow.66 This 
pooling of blood in the spinal veins increases epidural and intra -
thecal pressures, leading to an ischemic neuritis, which leads to 
the typical neurogenic claudication symptoms of stenosis. The 
arterial insufficiency theory of spinal stenosis is based on the arte-
rial dilatation of the lumbar radicular vessels during lower limb 
exercise to provide increased blood flow and nourishment to the 
nerve roots. In patients with spinal stenosis, this reflex dilatation 
might be defective.
14 Because patients with spinal stenosis are 
typically older adults, they are also at higher risk for atherosclero-
sis, which in turn amplifies the arterial insufficiency.
Neurogenic claudication is the most common presenting 
symptom of lumbar stenosis and results from central canal nar -
rowing. It is classically described as back and bilateral leg pain 
initiated by walking, prolonged standing, and walking downhill 
(relative lumbar extension). It is typically relieved by sitting or 
bending forward. If foraminal or lateral recess stenosis is the pri -
mary pathologic issue, then patients can present with more stan -
dard radicular pain in the typical dermatomal distribution. Most 
patients default to standing or walking in a forward-flexed posture 
(the grocery cart sign) to widen the central canal. Although this 
posture reduces mechanical compression of the nerve roots, it can 
lead to substantial hip flexion contractures. The variable symp -
toms of spinal stenosis are due to the fact that one or more nerve 
roots can be affected at multiple locations within the lumbar 
spine. Nerve compression can result from narrowing of the central 
canal, lateral recess, or intervertebral foramina. This can lead to a 
range of symptoms, from monoradiculopathy to polyradiculopa -
thy, and the characteristic signs of neurogenic claudication.  
Diagnosis is usually made based on clinical presentation, MRI 
findings, and sometimes electrodiagnosis. Electrodiagnostic stud-
ies are often performed in patients with suspected spinal stenosis 
to rule out other reasons for leg symptoms (such as peripheral 
neuropathy), and they can be helpful in fully characterizing nerve 
root involvement. Box 34.6 gives a classification schema for spinal 
stenosis, and Table 34.7 outlines a radiologic grading scale.

SECTION 4  Issues in Specific Diagnoses
The natural history of lumbar spinal stenosis is favorable. 
Johnsson et al. 160 followed patients with lumbar stenosis over a 
4-year period with conservative treatment. Based on subjective 
patient reports, 70% remained unchanged, 15% improved, and 
15% worsened. Walking capacity improved in 42% of patients, 
remained unchanged in 32%, and decreased in 26%. Amundsen 
et al.
8 reported on a 10-year study of patients randomly assigned 
to surgical or nonsurgical treatment. Nonsurgical treatment con -
sisted of bracing for 1 month followed by physical therapy. They 
demonstrated that neurologic deterioration was rare, that delay-
ing surgery (with conservative management) had no effect on 
postoperative outcomes, and that, at 4 years, half the conservative 
treatment group and four-fifths of the surgical group had favor -
able outcomes. During the final 6 years of study, clinical deterio -
ration of symptoms was rare. In general, most patients with 
symptomatic stenosis remained unchanged.
The primary goals of conservative management are pain con -
trol and reduction in the functional limitations that result from 
the reduced activity. Management programs typically include ex -
ercise, oral medications, and epidural steroids. There is a paucity 
of studies examining specific rehabilitation protocols. Flexion-
based lumbar stabilization exercises are typically used, which in-
clude strengthening the abdominals and pelvic girdle stabilizers, 
including the gluteal muscles. Improving hip mobility through 
stretching, especially of the anterior muscles (iliopsoas and rectus 
femoris), is also key. Aerobic conditioning is the final component 
of a comprehensive rehabilitation program for stenosis.
The oral medications used to manage pain in lumbar stenosis 
are the same as those previously mentioned for treating radicu -
lopathy. However, extra caution should be taken regarding poten-
tial side effects because most patients with lumbar stenosis are 
older and often have multiple medical conditions requiring several 
medications. ESIs are commonly used to manage symptoms, and 
a recent systematic review showed short-term improvement in pain 
and walking tolerance after ESIs, although it also highlighted the 
lack of strong supporting evidence in the literature.
33 Another 
study found that performing anesthetic epidural injections with or 
without steroid results in similar effects on pain and function, with 
reduction in use of opioids and spine surgery.
108,109 See the proce-
dures section for further details.
Surgery for lumbar stenosis is often reserved for patients with 
intractable pain resistant to nonoperative management, profound 
or progressive neurologic deficit, or lifestyle impairment. Surgical 
literature supports decompression for spinal stenosis, but meth -
odologic flaws make it difficult to definitively conclude whether 
surgical care is superior to conservative treatment.
388 If the primary 
symptom is axial LBP rather than leg pain, surgical outcomes are 
generally poorer.
172 Laminectomies are the most common decom-
pressive procedures.172,293,296,388 Fusion is often performed when 
spinal stenosis is associated with instability, degenerative spondylo-
listhesis, deformity, or recurrent stenosis. Instrumentation often 
improves the fusion rate but does not influence the clinical out -
comes. Surgical treatment has a much higher complication rate 
(10% to 24%) compared with conservative care.
388
Spondyloarthropathies
Spondyloarthropathies are a group of diseases associated with the 
human leukocyte antigen (HLA)–B27 allele. They are also 
termed seronegative spondyloarthropathies because patients  
generally have negative antinuclear antibodies and rheumatoid 
factor. They include ankylosing spondylitis, reactive arthritis, 
psoriatic arthritis, enteropathic arthritis, and undifferentiated 
spondyloarthropathy. It is hypothesized that in genetically  
susceptible individuals, an interplay of environmental and  
immunologic factors leads to the clinical manifestation of dis -
ease. Although the diseases are grouped together, each has unique 
features on clinical presentation.
169
Ankylosing Spondylitis
Ankylosing spondylitis is the prototype for spondyloarthropa -
thies. It is three times more common in males than in females, 
with symptoms beginning in the late teens or 20s. In general, 
ankylosing spondylitis presents with morning stiffness and a dull 
ache in the low back or buttocks. Common physical exam find-
ings include decreased spinal mobility, decreased chest expansion, 
and tenderness of the sacroiliac joints with direct pressure and 
with maneuvers that stress the joints.
169
Findings outside the spine are common, with hip or shoulder 
arthritis occurring in about 30% of patients, as well as asymmetric 
peripheral joint arthritis. Bony tenderness and enthesitis at mul-
tiple sites, such as the heels, greater trochanters, iliac crests, and 
tibial tuberosities, are also common. Systemic manifestations can 
include anterior uveitis, heart disease, and inflammatory bowel 
disease.
347
Radiographs of the lumbar spine may demonstrate a squaring 
of the VBs, eventually forming syndesmophytes and the appear -
ance of a bamboo spine. Sacroiliitis is typically bilateral and sym -
metric on radiographs, which can help to establish the diagnosis. 
However, changes may not appear until late in the disease. Find -
ings on MRI will appear much earlier in the course of the disease 
and have more recently been accepted to aid in a clinical diagnosis 
of ankylosing spondylitis.
20,293 The HLA-B27 gene is present in 
approximately 90% of patients with ankylosing spondylitis. 48 
Many patients also have an acute phase response, with elevated 
ESR or CRP .
Initial treatment focuses on patient education, encouraging 
regular exercise, and smoking cessation. Evidence demonstrates 
that exercise improves mobility, enhances function, and helps

relieve pain and inflammation so that the exercises can be per -
formed and function maintained. Indomethacin is particularly 
effective for this condition. Sulfasalazine may be considered in 
patients with peripheral arthritis. Disease-modifying agents, such 
as the tumor necrosis factor inhibitors, are also used to treat an -

activity despite conventional treatments. 353 These appear to be 
effective in controlling articular inflammation but have not been 
shown to prevent joint ankylosis.153 Image-guided sacroiliac joint 
injections can reduce symptoms acutely but do not have long-
term benefit.347
Other Spondyloarthropathies
Reactive arthritis (Reiter syndrome) can involve the spine, often 
presenting with asymmetric sacroiliitis and discontinuous spon -
dylitis. Symptoms typically follow a genitourinary or gastrointes -
tinal infection, with systemic symptoms being common.  
Conjunctivitis occurs in up to 50% of patients. Psoriatic arthritis 
can also affect the spine, but more commonly presents with an 
oligoarticular pattern involving the distal joints. Enteropathic 
spondyloarthropathies affect about 20% of patients with inflam -
matory bowel disease and can be clinically indistinguishable from 
ankylosing spondylitis. Finally, undifferentiated spondyloarthrop-
athies are diagnosed when a patient exhibits some features of 
spondyloarthropathies but does not meet the full diagnostic crite-
ria for a specific condition. From a rehabilitation perspective, 
treatment is similar to that for ankylosing spondylitis. 169
Nonlumbar Spine Causes of  
Radicular Leg Symptoms
A number of nonspinal disorders mimic lumbar radiculopathy 
because they generate pain referral patterns similar to lumbosacral 
dermatomes. Their etiology is diverse and includes joint, soft tis -
sue, vascular, and peripheral nerve sources. A thorough history 
and physical examination can typically help to differentiate these 
disorders from lumbosacral radiculopathy; however, other diag -
nostic studies might be necessary.
Joint Disorders
The sacroiliac joint is generally accepted as a potential pain gen -
erator that can refer pain into the lower limb. Other than true 
sacroiliitis (associated with the spondyloarthropathies), it is still 
not known whether it is the synovium, the articular cartilage, the 
capsule, the ligamentous structures, the muscular support of the 
sacroiliac joint, or a combination that is the source of pain.  
Although there are multiple physical examination maneuvers cre-
ated to stress the sacroiliac joint and reproduce pain, rigorous 
studies have demonstrated that no one physical examination  
maneuver (or combination) correlates well with a diagnosis of 
sacroiliac joint pain.88 The gold standard for diagnosing sacroiliac 
joint pain is a fluoroscopically guided injection of local anesthetic 
into the sacroiliac joint.
Guided injections have helped to delineate the sclerotomal 
referral pattern of pain emanating from the sacroiliac joint. 103,104 
Sacroiliac joint pain generally does not radiate above the lumbo -
sacral junction but can radiate into the groin, thigh, and even 
below the knee, with substantial overlap of lumbosacral radicular 
pain patterns. For more information, please review the section on 
interventional procedures involving the sacroiliac joint.
Disorders involving the hip joint generally refer pain into the 
groin and sometimes the anterior thigh. The prototypical disorder 
is osteoarthritis of the hip. This pain pattern is easily confused 
with L1, L2, or L3 nerve root involvement. Patients may also 
complain of posterior pelvic pain concomitant with groin pain.272 
Plain films of the hip and ROM testing on physical examination 
are generally most helpful in differentiating intraarticular hip pain 
from referred pain. An ultrasound or fluoroscopy-guided intraar -
ticular local anesthetic injection into the hip joint may further 
help to differentiate from extraarticular causes.
Soft Tissue Disorders
Piriformis syndrome is thought to cause sciatica from local pres -
sure on the sciatic nerve in the pelvis. Pain generally radiates into 
the posterior thigh but can refer below the knee in an L5 or S1 
dermatomal pattern. The patient will also describe buttock pain 
and typically has tenderness over the sciatic notch. Multiple ex -
amination maneuvers are used to reproduce sciatica resulting 
from piriformis syndrome. 19 The Pace maneuver is described as 
resisted abduction and external rotation of the thigh. The  
Freiberg maneuver is forceful internal rotation of the extended 
thigh. Beatty 19 described his maneuver as deep buttock pain 
produced by the side-lying patient holding a flexed knee several 
inches off the table. Fishman and Zybert 102 described an electro -
physiologic approach to diagnose piriformis syndrome using H 
waves. A single-center retrospective study conducted in 2019 
demonstrated ultrasound had the ability to serve as an alternative 
method for diagnosing piriformis syndrome, but the diagnosis is 
still largely clinical. 381
Greater trochanteric pain syndrome is a descriptive term for a 
regional pain syndrome focused about the greater trochanter,  
buttock, and lateral thigh.317 It is often initially diagnosed as tro -
chanteric bursitis but is probably multifactorial in etiology. An  
association probably exists with gluteal muscle (medius and mini-
mus) pathology, potentially tendinopathy, tears, or myofascial pain. 
Physical examination shows generalized tenderness in the region, 
and typically there is substantial gluteal muscle inhibition and de-
conditioning that can manifest as hip abductor weakness. A com-
prehensive rehabilitation program focusing initially on pain control 
and neuromuscular reeducation of the gluteal muscles is important 
before progressing to strength-building exercises for these muscles.
Iliotibial (IT) band syndrome can be confused with L4 or L5 
radicular pain. The IT band is an extension of the tensor fasciae 
latae that traverses the lateral aspect of the thigh, attaching to Gerdy 
tubercle on the proximal lateral tibia. IT band syndrome typically 
presents as lateral knee pain, but it can also present with more 
proximal (lateral thigh) pain or radiate distally into the calf. When 
the IT band is tight, it may also exacerbate greater trochanteric pain 
syndrome and can be associated with lateral hip and buttock pain. 
IT band tightness is evaluated with the Ober maneuver.217
Myofascial pain syndromes are common and thought to arise 
from active trigger points within a muscle or its fascia. 320 Activa-
tion of trigger points in various muscles has typical pain referral 
patterns that can mimic lumbosacral dermatomes. Please refer to 
the procedures section of the chapter for more information re -
garding this topic.
Vascular Disorders
Vascular claudication from peripheral vascular disease can be dif -
ficult to differentiate from neurogenic claudication secondary to

lumbar spinal stenosis, especially because both are common in 
older adult patients. Symptoms from both are exacerbated by 
walking; however, a major difference is that spinal forward flexion 
or sitting is necessary to alleviate the symptoms of neurogenic 
claudication. The pain of intermittent vascular claudication typi -
cally resolves when walking is discontinued, even if the patient 
remains standing. Leaning forward on a grocery cart or walker 
while ambulating can also reduce neurogenic claudication but 
does not help with vascular claudication. The bicycle test can be 
used to differentiate between the two because any lower limb  
exercise should exacerbate vascular claudication, but stationary 
cycling (while sitting with lumbar flexion) should not exacerbate 
neurogenic claudication. A patient with neurogenic claudication 
also typically has more pain with downhill walking because of the 
relative lumbar extension and resultant narrowing of the spinal 
canal. Uphill walking is more strenuous and can rapidly bring on 
symptoms of vascular disease.
Peripheral Nerve Disorders
Peripheral polyneuropathy is a common cause of paresthesias in 
the distal lower limbs and feet that can mimic symptoms of 
lumbar stenosis. They are often seen together in older adult pa -
tients with diabetes. Electrodiagnostic studies can be used to 
diagnose superimposed peripheral polyneuropathy in patients 
who have MRI findings of lumbar stenosis. Similarly, lateral 
femoral cutaneous mononeuropathy (meralgia paresthetica) can 
also mimic radiculopathy because it causes numbness in the 
lateral thigh.
Low Back Pain in Special Populations
Low Back Pain in Pregnancy
LBP is a common complaint in pregnancy. Patients are generally 
divided into two categories: those with LBP and those with pelvic 
girdle pain (PGP; pain below the iliac crest, such as sacroiliac 
joint–related pain). Multiple studies have estimated the preva -
lence of LBP in pregnancy at 49% to 76%. 95,192,256,259,369 Risk 
factors include a history of previous back pain, previous preg -
nancy-related back pain, and LBP during menses. 36,369 Exercise 
reduces the occurrence of pregnancy-related back pain and dis -
ability.208 LBP can begin at any time during the pregnancy and 
generally peaks at 36 weeks. 192,256,369 Pain decreases after this 
point and in most patients is substantially improved by 3 months 
postpartum.256
A small group of patients will have persistent pain even after 
postpartum recovery. Risk factors for persistent back pain after 
pregnancy include having both LBP and PGP , pain occurring in 
early pregnancy, weakness of back extensors, older patients, and 
those with work dissatisfaction.129
The etiology of LBP during pregnancy is thought to stem from 
a combination of increased biomechanical strain and altered hor -
monal influences. Biomechanical changes occur as the pregnant 
female’s center of gravity shifts forward, leading to alterations in 
spinal posture. However, evidence against purely biomechanical 
factors being the primary cause is that the pain often begins before 
significant maternal weight gain, and its incidence does not con -
sistently correlate with weight gain.192 Hormonal influences likely 
play a role as well, as pregnancy-related hormonal changes can 
affect the lumbopelvic ligaments, reducing the stability of the 
lumbosacral spine and making it more susceptible to stress. 192
A direct correlation between circulating levels of the hormone 
relaxin and pelvic and back pain is controversial. 5,135,193,216 The 
prevalence of disc abnormalities on MRI is the same for pregnant 
and nonpregnant females; thus this may be a source of pain for 
some pregnant females. 374 Only a few high-quality studies have 
evaluated therapeutic interventions in pregnancy-related LBP .339 
Individualized physical therapy, water aerobics, acupuncture, and 
massage therapy can be recommended to decrease pain.99,180,250,373 
Instruction on a home exercise program, use of a sacroiliac belt, 
and back school have not been shown to significantly decrease 
pain intensity.88,226,235,248
A full discussion regarding the use of medication during preg -
nancy is beyond the scope of this chapter. In general, any medica-
tion prescribed should be discussed with the patient’s obstetrician. 
Even medications generally thought of as safe and well tolerated 
can have unexpected consequences during pregnancy. For exam -
ple, the use of NSAIDs in late pregnancy can cause premature 
closure of the ductus arteriosus and neonatal renal failure.266 Most 
antidepressants have not been approved for use during pregnancy, 
and for most antiseizure medications, such as gabapentin, there is 
evidence for increased incidence of birth defects in animals, and 
they have not been well studied in humans. 167
Bracing during pregnancy can be challenging due to factors 
such as patient comfort, changing size of the gravid uterus, mate-
rials used, and specific location of pain. Bertuit et al., in a 2018 
study, explored the effectiveness of two different types of belts. In 
this study, females wore the belts during daily activities, outings, 
and walking. Results showed that 48% of participants experi -
enced pain relief, while 63% reported improved support.  
Although there were no significant differences in overall pain re -
lief between the two belt types, the study found that narrower 
belts significantly reduced pain intensity (p 5 0.001) and pain at 
the sacroiliac joint (p 5 0.003). In contrast, wider belts were 
more effective in reducing spinal pain (p 5 0.01).22 In addition, 
PGP is also a common occurrence in pregnancy with peak inci -
dence between 24 and 36 weeks of gestation and may be related 
to biomechanical alteration, sacroiliac joint laxity, and reduction 
in stability.54 Fig. 34.22 shows common braces available during 
pregnancy and postpartum.
Pediatric Low Back Pain
Back pain in children was once considered rare and often raised 
concerns about serious underlying conditions, but this belief is no 
longer felt to be valid. Like adults, 80% of pediatric back pain 
cases lack a definitive diagnosis, making it a common issue among 
children. Studies involving over 300 children have reported back 
pain prevalence rates between 30% and 51%. 16,125 Severe back 
pain, characterized by either recurring or persistent discomfort, 
has been reported in 3% to 15% of cases. The prevalence of back 
pain tends to increase with age. For example, a Finnish cohort 
study found that back pain affected 1% of 7-year-olds, 6% of 
10-year-olds, and 18% of 14-year-olds.345 Another study reported 
that 12% of 11-year-olds and 50% of adolescents aged 15 to  
18 experienced back pain, approaching the prevalence seen in 
adults.39 This study also noted that while back pain is often recur-
rent, it is frequently forgotten over time. Other research indicates 
that the prevalence of LBP increases most significantly during 
puberty and periods of rapid growth.200,360
Risk factors for nonspecific LBP in children and adolescents 
include increasing age, female gender, a family history of LBP , 
depression, participation in competitive sports, and both very

high and very low levels of physical activity. 214 Sitting appears to 
be the main exacerbating factor of LBP in the pediatric popula-
tion.15 There also appears to be a positive correlation between 
LBP in adolescence and the presence of pain as an adult. 137
Recent attention has been given to the potential role of back -
pack use in the development of pediatric LBP , though no defini-
tive causation has been established. Carrying a backpack that  
exceeds 7.5% to 15% of the wearer’s body weight significantly 
increases the metabolic demands beyond those required for mov -
ing one’s own body weight.
215,287 The general recommendation 
for a child’s backpack weight is limited to 10% of body weight.215 
This limit is based on concerns of increasing metabolic costs and 
not on the risk of back pain development (there are conflicting 
reports in the literature regarding backpack weight and back 
pain).
130,154,215,351,370 Many new backpack designs improve ergo -
nomic fit; however, there are no studies demonstrating their  
effectiveness in reducing back pain.154
There is currently no evidence that structural spine deformity 
can result from backpack use.64
Some common causes of LBP in the pediatric population are 
listed in Box 34.7. Spondylolysis and isthmic spondylolisthesis are 
often present in young athletes with back pain and have been re -
ported as the most common identifiable cause of persistent LBP 
among children and adolescents.
214,237 Most believe the etiology is 
from overuse, particularly during the growth spurt. The presence 
of isthmic defects in children in the Western world is between 2% 
and 7% and as high as 30% in elite athletes.
255
Scheuermann disease typically presents in adolescence as pain-
less exaggerated thoracic kyphosis. From a postural standpoint, 
the teenager typically presents with excessive thoracic kyphosis 
(which is demonstrated to be fixed in attempted hyperextension), 
with a compensatory lumbar hyperlordosis. Radiographic  
criteria for the diagnosis of Scheuermann disease include  
anterior wedging of at least three adjacent vertebrae, end-plate 
irregularities, Schmorl nodes, and discal space narrowing.

These findings are present equally in the adolescent population 
without back pain, but there is a higher prevalence of concomi -
tant degenerative disc changes in those with pain.348 The etiology 
of Scheuermann disease is uncertain. Some believe that it is  
attributable to repetitive loading of the immature spine that 
might have some preexisting abnormality of the cartilaginous 
end plate.
149 There does appear to be a familial link. 233 Scheuer-
mann disease can have a benign course, although some untreated 
patients develop progressive structural kyphosis. Wearing a brace 
is recommended until skeletal maturity is reached to help prevent 
progressive kyphosis.
Idiopathic scoliosis is generally not painful. Scoliosis is a three-
dimensional deformity, defined by spinal curves exceeding  
10 degrees in the posteroanterior plane on radiography. Idio -
pathic scoliosis accounts for approximately 80% of structural 
coronal deformities. There is no difference in the prevalence of 
back pain or mortality between patients with untreated adolescent 
idiopathic scoliosis and the general population.
The curve direction in idiopathic scoliosis is typically right tho-
racic and left lumbar. If an atypical curve is encountered, further 
evaluation beyond plain films is generally warranted. If scoliosis

is associated with pain, often there is a more serious underlying 
pathology. These presentations are evaluated with careful musculo-
skeletal and neurologic evaluations to identify any neuromuscular 
causes. MRI of the spine is essential in evaluating for potential 
causes such as tumors, infections, and spondylolisthesis. Manage-
ment of pain in the setting of scoliosis is tailored to individual 
presentations and includes treating the underlying cause, with 
consideration for bracing and surgery to prevent or address any 
associated neurologic deficits.
Fortunately, neoplastic disease of the pediatric spine is rare. 
Most pediatric spinal tumors are primary (not metastatic) benign 
bone tumors arising from the vertebrae. 149 The most common  
tumors of the pediatric spine include osteoid osteoma, osteoblas -
toma, and aneurysmal bone cysts. The classic pain of osteoid  
osteoma occurs nocturnally and responds to aspirin. The most 
frequent malignant lesion affecting the pediatric spine is Ewing 
sarcoma.
Treatment of Low Back Pain
Most treatment guidelines use the biopsychosocial framework 
to guide multimodal, interdisciplinary treatment plans. Unfor -
tunately, most studies on the various treatment options for LBP , 
particularly chronic LBP , show limited efficacy. Large trials on 
the most commonly prescribed treatments, such as medications 
and exercise, tend to show only small improvements (i.e.,  
10 to 20 points on a 100-point pain visual analogue scale) over 
placebo.38 Given that LBP is often multifactorial in etiology 
and individual treatments have limited benefit, most clinicians 
employ a multimodal approach to treatment in hopes that the 
cumulative effect of multiple treatments will lead to significant 
improvements in pain and function. Evidence-based practices 
recommend that first-line treatments include patient education 
on self-management, return to normal activities and exercise, 
physical therapy, certain movement-based therapies, and psy -
chological programs for those with persistent symptoms. Less 
emphasis is placed on bedrest, pharmacologic treatments, spi -
nal injections, and surgery. 105 The most common treatments for 
LBP are discussed next.
Multidisciplinary Biopsychosocial Rehabilitation
Chronic LBP significantly impairs physical, psychological, and 
social functioning. Therefore effective treatment must address 
all these dimensions comprehensively. A biopsychosocial ap -
proach is crucial for achieving successful, sustained improve -
ments in chronic LBP , as psychological and social factors are 
thought to contribute to the development and persistence of 
pain and disability. 164 A large body of literature highlights the 
complex cultural, psychological, social support, and economic 
factors that influence pain and rehabilitation outcomes. The 
biopsychosocial approach to treatment has led to the develop -
ment of multidisciplinary biopsychosocial rehabilitation (MBR) 
programs, which involve collaboration of a multidisciplinary 
team (as detailed in the chronic pain chapter and Table 38.5) to 
enhance patient function, reduce pain, and improve quality of 
life. A Cochrane review and meta-analysis concluded MBR in -
terventions are more effective than standard care with isolated 
treatments (such as exercise therapy, behavioral treatments, 
patient education, and physical condition programs for  
improving work outcome) in reducing pain and disability for 
patients with chronic LBP .165
Patient Education
Education is consistently recommended as part of an evidence-
based, ethical, and patient-centered approach to the management 
of LBP .326 Given that back pain is the leading cause of disability 
and considering the complex interplay between pain and patients’ 
beliefs about it, along with the often misleading and commercially 
influenced information widely available, the role of a physician in 
providing clear, accurate patient education is crucial. When ap -
propriate, reassurance should be offered that there is no serious 
underlying pathology and that symptoms are likely to improve 
with time. Reassurance, along with encouragement to avoid bed -
rest, stay active, and continue participation in usual activities in -
cluding work, can help counter misinformation, fear avoidance, 
and catastrophizing thoughts.105,366 There is strong evidence that 
continuing activity as normally as possible expedites recovery and 
can lead to less disability. 105,387 Providing empathy and a strong 
therapeutic alliance improves adherence to treatments and leads 
to better outcomes.366
Education about recovery expectation, self-management strat -
egies, and maintenance of usual activities should be conveyed to 
the patient in a digestible manner at the appropriate level of 
health care literacy. A study on patients with LBP in a primary 
care setting found that patients want information from their phy-
sicians about the expected course of their pain, strategies for 
managing it, how to resume normal activities quickly, and ways to 
reduce the frequency and severity of future episodes. These topics 
were ranked higher in importance than receiving a diagnosis for 
their pain.365 That being said, addressing any misconceptions re -
garding diagnosis and treatment can be of key importance. For 
example, a survey study on patients presenting to an outpatient 
neurosurgery clinic with spondylosis with radiculopathy found 
more than two-thirds (67%) believed MRI is more important 
than clinical findings in the decision-making process for their 
condition. Furthermore, nearly half (47%) said they would be 
willing to undergo surgery based on abnormal MRI findings 
alone even if they had no or few symptoms. 371 Unsurprisingly, 
most patients appear to have a biomedical understanding of ra -
dicular pain, and that perspective is shared by many other health 
care professionals and society at large. Physicians must carefully 
encourage an evidence-based, less fear-inducing understanding of 
these conditions while ensuring they do not downplay the pa -
tient’s concerns.159 Low health literacy has been linked to higher 
levels of LBP; therefore communication between the clinician and 
the patient should be adjusted to the individual’s prior knowledge 
and beliefs when possible.123
It is crucial to explain pain in a way that helps patients under -
stand its biologic causes while also reducing fear and anxiety, 
which should engender improvements in pain and disability. 243 
For example, research suggests that “pinched” nerve is probably 
nocebic, so “compressed” or “crowded” is a more favorable and 
accurate description.159 A randomized controlled study has also 
demonstrated that providing educational material via metaphor 
and story facilitates the patient’s ability to reconceptualize pain 
and reduce catastrophizing.117
Exercise Therapy
Exercise is a widely effective intervention to prevent and treat 
LBP .141,333 Most clinical practice guidelines, including the 
American College of Physicians (ACP) and the UK National 
Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE), recommend

731Chapter 34 Low Back Disorders
nonpharmacologic approaches with exercise and physical ther -
apy as first-line treatments. 141,184 A 2021 Cochrane review on 
exercise for the treatment of chronic LBP concluded with mod -
erate certainty that exercise treatment is more effective than no 
treatment, standard care by a family physician, or placebo for 
pain intensity and functional limitations outcomes. 141 Exercise 
treatments aim to reduce pain and improve muscle and joint 
strength, function, and ROM. 141
Exercise therapy for LBP reduces pain, improves function, 
decreases catastrophizing, and results in additional physiologic 
and psychological benefits from exercise. Depending on the type 
of exercise, these activities may also improve cardiovascular 
health, muscle mass, weight, depression, and sleep. 173,249 Even 
small amounts of exercise that are not sufficient to cause physio -
logic changes have been found to improve pain and function; 
these effects are mediated through reducing fear avoidance and 
anxiety. When patients who are fearful of painful physical activity 
gradually increase their tolerance for activity and persist through 
pain/pain tolerance, there is a resultant reduction in fear of move-
ment, reinjury, and catastrophizing.27
Despite good evidence that exercise is effective for the treat -
ment of LBP , studies have yet to discern the type, duration, or 
frequency of exercise that is best.141 Guidelines recommend exer-
cise programs that account for the individual patient’s needs, 
preferences, comorbidities, and capabilities because of the lack of 
evidence showing one form of exercise is better than another. 105 
Early supervised physical therapy programs may be unnecessary 
for many patients, especially those who present with an acute 
episode of LBP without red flags/risk factors/weakness, because 
they are most likely to self-resolve within 6 weeks. However, 
guided exercise programs should be considered for patients at risk 
for persistent and debilitating pain. For example, a graded activity 
program targeting improvements in function and disability would 
be highly appropriate for patients limited by fear avoidance and 
catastrophizing thoughts. 105 Formal exercise programs may also 
be necessary for patients with physical limitations from their 
various medical comorbidities. For example, patients with poor 
cardiovascular tolerance or at high fall risk may require 1:1 super-
vised sessions to safely participate in graded exercise treatment 
programs. Aquatic therapy may be more appropriate for patients 
who are unable to tolerate land-based exercises.
Traditional Physical Therapy
Formal physical therapy programs often target lumbar stabiliza -
tion. Exercise interventions for LBP most commonly focus on 
improving strength and flexibility of the core, back, and hip mus-
culature.141,257 A popular approach is the use of motor control 
exercise (MCE) to restore control and coordination of the deep 
trunk muscles, which is thought to improve the stability and 
control of the spine.298 MCE programs usually focus on strength-
ening the deep spine stabilizers, such as the multifidi and trans -
versus abdominus. T reatment progresses to more functional  
activities and advances from static exercises to more complex, 
dynamic tasks that integrate the activation of the deep and global 
trunk muscles. 298 Often, supervised exercise programs are pre -
scribed so that the patient can correctly learn how to perform 
therapeutic activities. In individual treatment sessions with MCE, 
physical therapists often use biofeedback (through palpation or 
ultrasound imaging) to confirm correct activation of the trunk 
muscles and identify any incorrect overactivation of the superficial 
trunk muscles.298 The formality of supervised exercise programs 
may also facilitate adherence. Poor adherence is a large barrier to 
successful exercise treatment, and physicians should highlight the 
importance of a daily home exercise program. 298
The McKenzie Method
The McKenzie method is another common approach that physi -
cal therapists use when treating LBP , especially if there is a  
concomitant radicular component. This method involves an indi-
vidualized assessment to categorically attribute back pain to  
derangement, dysfunction, and postural syndrome. The largest 
culprit is thought to be derangement, and extension-based load -
ing exercises are selected that move or centralize the radicular pain 
into the axial back. 141 Postural training and education are also 
taught to facilitate pain relief through self-management.
Aquatic Therapy
Aquatic physical therapy is a form of low-impact exercise treat -
ment that should be considered for patients who are unable to 
tolerate land-based therapies. Water-based exercise naturally 
offloads weight-bearing joints; and the buoyancy of water makes 
it easier for individuals, who are limited by pain, weakness, body 
habitus, or mobility issues, to exercise. In addition to buoyancy, 
aquatic therapy has intrinsic therapeutic properties, including 
density, thermal capacity, and conductivity. 263 The resistance of 
water slows movements, giving individuals better motor control, 
reducing risk of injury, and possibly reducing fear avoidance. In 
addition, the sensory input from water (i.e., temperature, hydro -
static pressure, and turbulence) may reduce pain via the gate 
theory. Warm water can help relax muscles, reduce spasms, and 
alleviate myofascial pain. Aquatic therapy is also considered ben -
eficial for improving cardiovascular fitness, balance, coordination, 
and flexibility. Multiple reviews have established that aquatic ex -
ercise programs are effective for the treatment of chronic LBP .212,263
Weight Loss
Weight loss is widely recommended as part of a comprehensive 
treatment plan for overweight or obese patients with chronic 
LBP . Aquatic therapy may be considered as an alternative form of 
exercise therapy for patients with LBP who find it difficult to 
tolerate land-based exercises due to physical limitations from 
obesity. Numerous studies have established a significant associa -
tion between LBP and elevated BMI. 201,290 Studies attribute up 
to 45% of the burden of osteoarthritis and back pain to obe -
sity.289 Overweight or obese patients, particularly those with 
central obesity, likely have a moderately elevated risk (1.4  
increased odds) of developing persistent back pain. 234,289,290 Lit-
erature also suggests obese patients with LBP have increased dis -
ability, more severe pain, and worse functional status than their 
nonobese counterparts. 290 A study on morbidly obese patients 
who underwent bariatric surgery found significant improvements 
in functional status, with 65% of patients reporting complete 
resolution of LBP within 2 years. 234,290 Another study on a simi -
lar population demonstrated weight loss after bariatric surgery 
was associated with a marked increase in the L4-L5 IVD height 
on imaging, along with a clinically significant decrease in axial 
low back and radicular pain. 207 T raditionally, nonsurgical weight 
loss through lifestyle changes (i.e. diet and exercise) is challeng -
ing due to its reliance on behavioral modification; however, the 
advent of weight loss drugs, such as glucagon-like peptide-1

viable treatment option in the future for the subset of patients 
with obesity and LBP who are appropriate candidates for  
medication-assisted weight loss interventions.

Movement-Based Therapies
Movement therapy refers to a broad range of Eastern and West -
ern mind-body practices designed to improve mental, physical, 
and spiritual health concurrently ( Table 34.8). 267 Movement 
therapies used in combination with conventional medical treat -
ments allow physicians to offer patient-centered, integrative care 
that accounts for the physical, psychological, social, and spiritual 
aspects of health and wellness. Studies demonstrate broad bene-
fits of movement-based therapies, including reductions in pain 
and stress, and improvements in debility, mobility, strength, bal -
ance, coordination, cardiovascular fitness, mood, and cognition. 
Moreover, international guidelines across specialties recommend 
these nonpharmacologic holistic treatment options. For example, 
the ACP clinical practice guidelines strongly recommend exer -
cises, multidisciplinary rehabilitation, mindfulness-based stress 
reduction, tai chi, yoga, motor control relaxation, or progressive

improves pain tolerance. Compared to controls, yoga practitioners:
Tolerated pain more than twice as long as controls
Had more gray matter in areas uniquely correlated to pain tolerance and areas  
responsible for integrating nociceptive input and parasympathetic activation
n This use-dependent hypertrophy suggests that a regular yoga practice  
improves pain tolerance by teaching ways to deal with sensory inputs and  
the potential emotional reactions associated with those inputs.
A randomized controlled trial (RCT) on yoga for low back pain also highlighted  
yoga’s impact on neuromodulation. Yoga led to:
Decreased pain scores
Improved range of motion
Higher levels of serotonin and brain-derived neurotrophic factor (known modula -
tors of nociception)
Pilates A low-impact practice that uses isokinetic exercises with 
resistance to strengthen deep muscle groups.
Based in holistic movement principles, including concentra-
tion, centering, control, breathing, precision, and flow
Evidence suggests Pilates effectively treats low back pain using similar strategies 
as conventional evidence-based physical therapy programs. Pilates strength -
ens deep core musculature and focuses on precise body/posture alignment 
and awareness, resulting in a physique that mediates low back pain.
Studies show resultant physical changes from Pilates include:
Increased rectus abdominis strength
Elimination of muscular asymmetries in transversus abdominis and obliques
Improved isolation of transversus abdominis
Improved spinal stability with limb loading
Improved hamstring flexibility
Improved abdominal muscular endurance
Another RCT comparing Pilates to aerobic exercise suggested that Pilates is a 
more effective treatment for low back pain and disability because the exercise 
targets the muscles of the pelvis and trunk.
Tai chi A meditative martial arts practice designed to gently 
strengthen and relax the body and mind.
It is a system featuring coordinated movements, meditation, 
and purposeful breathing that is believed to help restore 
the body’s qi (the energy source that is believed to be 
responsible for physical, mental, and spiritual health).
This integrative approach is recommended in current guidelines for reducing 
chronic general musculoskeletal pain and associated disability.
Its advantage could be attributed to the biopsychosocial care tai chi offers over 
conventional aerobic exercise.
The highest quality evidence supports tai chi for fall prevention in community-
dwelling elderly.
Qigong Similar to tai chi, qigong uses the “mind” (or concentration) 
to coordinate breathing and smooth movements that  
promote the circulation of qi.
Tai chi and qigong practices are so similar that they are often grouped together in  
research studies as tai chi and qigong (TCQ). Similarly, systematic reviews on qigong 
establish comparable improvements in fall risk, depression, and quality of life.
Felden-
krais
Uses movement exploration to facilitate somatic learning for 
the reeducation of movement patterns.
Through a series of movements, the practitioner uses body sen-
sation and perceptual feedback to choose between favor-
able (easy, comfortable) and unfavorable (painful, straining) 
positions. With repetition, the practitioner develops discern-
ment between favorable and unfavorable movements; 
movement modifications develop and become engrained.
Although often compared with TCQ, the benefits of Feldenkrais are less estab -
lished in the literature.
A RCT suggests that Feldenkrais may reduce chronic tension-based pain. Felden -
krais resulted in more relaxed supine posture due to changes in muscle tone.
Another RCT poses that Feldenkrais has comparable efficacy to back school for 
the treatment of chronic low back pain.

relaxation for the initial treatment of chronic LBP . Additionally, 
NICE guidelines recommend yoga, Pilates, tai chi, and Felden -
krais as exercise therapies. 267 A systematic review also concluded 
that yoga, Pilates, and tai chi significantly improved chronic LBP 
compared with conventional rehabilitation and no interven-
tion.
205 As movement therapies gain mainstream acceptance, 
some health insurance plans have started to acknowledge their 
cost effectiveness and subsidize them as part of preventive care. 
Additionally, movement-based therapies can provide an alterna-
tive treatment option for those seeking to avoid pharmacologic 
interventions.
Movement-based therapies are generally well tolerated across 
diverse patient populations. These treatments can be tailored to an 
individual’s specific needs and goals while reducing kinesiopho -
bia, facilitating behavioral change, and providing another ap-
proach to health and well-being. These integrative modalities  
encourage successful, sustainable lifestyle changes by empowering 
individuals to take a proactive role in their own health as well as 
cultivating community support and accountability. Movement-
based therapy is often used in conjunction with other therapies 
and treatments to provide a holistic approach to recovery and 
wellness.
267
Modalities
A multimodal treatment approach to LBP is recommended by 
most clinical guidelines due to the complex nature of LBP , involv-
ing not just physical pathology but also psychological and social 
factors. Studies illustrate that combining different modalities  
often leads to better outcomes, rather than relying on a singular 
treatment alone.
105,184,387 By incorporating various nonpharmaco-
logic treatments, a multimodal approach can reduce the need for 
pain medications and their associated risks. Some commonly used 
nonpharmacologic modalities are described in Table 34.9.
184
Pharmacology
Medications are the most commonly used treatment for LBP , and 
common options include NSAIDs, acetaminophen, muscle  
relaxants, benzodiazepines, antidepressants, anticonvulsants, cor-
ticosteroids, and opioids (a comprehensive list of adjuvant medi -
cations commonly used for chronic pain can also be found in the 
chronic pain chapter and Table 38.6).
45 Despite their widespread 
use, the actual clinical effectiveness of these medications is modest 
and typically results in a 5% to 20% reduction in pain on visual 
TABLE 
34.9 Modalities
Technique Brief Description
Massage Deep tissue massage: Targets deeper layers of muscles to relieve chronic tension and pain.
Trigger point therapy: Focuses on specific points of muscle tightness that can refer pain to other areas of the body.
Dry needling or trigger point  
injections
Dry needling inserts a needle into specific trigger points or areas of tight muscle. The needle may be gently manipulated 
to elicit a twitch response from the muscle, which is believed to help release muscle tension and break the pain cycle. 
Unlike trigger point injections, dry needling does not involve the use of medications.
Trigger point injections involve injecting a small amount of medication (e.g., lidocaine) directly into a trigger point in the 
muscle to alleviate pain and reduce muscle tension.
Manual manipulation Applies force to specific joints in the spine, particularly in the lower back, with the goal of restoring normal motion and 
alignment. The manipulation can vary from gentle pressure to a more forceful thrust, depending on the practitioner’s  
assessment and the patient’s condition.
Traction Gently stretches the spine to relieve pressure on the discs and nerves, which can help with conditions such as herniated 
discs.
Chiropractor Spinal manipulation: Adjustments to the spine to improve alignment, reduce pain, and enhance function.
Mobilization: Gentle movement of the joints to improve flexibility and reduce pain.
Transcutaneous electrical nerve 
stimulation
Uses electrical currents to disrupt pain signals and stimulate the release of endorphins.
Heat and cold therapy Heat therapy: Application of heat (e.g., heating pads, warm baths) to relax muscles and increase blood flow.
Cold therapy: Application of cold (e.g., ice packs) to reduce inflammation and numb pain.
Ultrasound therapy Deep tissue heating: Uses sound waves to deliver deep heat to muscles and tissues, helping to reduce pain and increase 
mobility. Can also be used to allow for deeper penetration of topical medications.
Acupuncture/acupressure Needling: Insertion of acupuncture needles into specific points on the body to stimulate healing and reduce pain.
Electroacupuncture: A mild electrical current is applied to the needles to enhance effects.
Acupressure: A technique similar to acupuncture but without the use of needles. Instead, it involves applying pressure to 
specific points on the body, known as acupoints, to relieve pain, reduce tension, and promote healing.
Mind-body techniques Cognitive behavioral therapy: Helps patients manage pain by changing their perception and response to pain.
Mindfulness and meditation: Techniques to reduce stress by focusing on the present moment, reducing the tendency to 
catastrophize or overfocus on pain, which can lower pain intensity.
Biofeedback: A therapeutic technique used to help individuals gain control over certain physiologic functions that are  
typically involuntary, such as muscle tension. It is often used to manage and reduce pain, including low back pain, by 
teaching people how to regulate their body’s responses to pain and stress.

analog scale when compared to placebo. An overview paper on 
Cochrane reviews for LBP pharmacologic treatments found no 
high- or moderate-certainty evidence that pharmacologic inter -
vention provides a large or medium effect on pain intensity for 
acute or chronic LBP when compared to placebo. 45 Moreover, 
another systematic review found a clinically meaningful reduction 
in pain scores associated with the use of sham oral medications for 
LBP , suggesting a significant placebo effect. 175 Therefore, when 
selecting medications for LBP , it is useful to prioritize the safety 
profile of each medication employed, given limited benefit. Fur -
ther, the placebo effect can often provide substantial relief itself. 
Pharmacologic intervention may be most appropriate for patients 
who are not good candidates for interventional procedures due to 
high-risk comorbidities or those with widespread pain that cannot 
be addressed by targeted treatments.
NSAIDs
NSAIDs inhibit cyclooxygenase (COX) enzymes to reduce the 
production of proinflammatory mediators, called prostaglandins, 
resulting in a prominent antiinflammatory effect that yields an -
algesia.45 NSAIDs likely offer a modest but meaningful reduction 
in both acute and chronic LBP .45,90 The ACP guideline for LBP 
recommends NSAIDs as first-line pharmacologic treatment for 
acute or subacute LBP .184 Studies comparing different NSAIDs 
have not identified any particular NSAID to be superior to oth -
ers. If one NSAID class does not work, it is advisable to try a 
different class before concluding that a patient has failed NSAID 
therapy. An adequate NSAID trial is defined as 2 weeks at the 
maximum scheduled dose. Adverse effects associated with 
NSAIDs include gastrointestinal bleeding, renal impairment, 
and mildly prolonged bleeding time. Longer term use of NSAIDs 
has been associated with increased risk of thrombotic events such 
as myocardial ischemia and stroke in patients with cardiovascular 
disease.45
Acetaminophen
Acetaminophen also acts on the COX enzyme to provide analge -
sia but does not have antiinflammatory properties. The Cochrane 
overview found high-certainty evidence that acetaminophen does 
not reduce pain intensity or disability associated with acute LBP .45 
However, it is often combined synergistically with opioids. It also 
does not cause the side effects associated with NSAIDs (gastroin -
testinal ulceration, renal impairment, platelet impairment).
Muscle Relaxants
Muscle relaxants are a chemically varied group of medications 
that share a similar function. There are two main categories of 
muscle relaxants, antispasmodics and antispastics. Antispasmod -
ics are used to treat muscle spasms and myofascial pain associated 
with injury, whereas antispastics are used to treat spasticity with 
increased muscle tone.45 Muscle relaxants were found to be mildly 
effective for acute LBP treatment but were associated with more 
side effects.45 The ACP guidelines recommend muscle relaxant as 
pharmacologic treatment for acute or subacute LBP .184 Similar to 
NSAIDs, there is no evidence that one type of muscle relaxer is 
more efficacious than another.
Each muscle relaxant has distinct mechanisms of action and 
associated side effects, though fatigue, dizziness, and nausea are 
common across all muscle relaxants. Notably, cyclobenzaprine is 
chemically analogous to TCAs and could potentially contribute to 
serotonin syndrome when combined with other serotonergic 
medications. Also, certain muscle relaxants, such as carisoprodol, 
carry a high risk of misuse and dependency and are often recom -
mended against in many guidelines.
Antidepressants
Some antidepressants, such as TCAs and serotonin-norepineph -
rine reuptake inhibitors, are thought to provide analgesic effect 
independent of their effects on depression.45
Tricyclic Antidepressants. This older class of antidepressants 
works by increasing the amount of norepinephrine and serotonin 
in the spinal cord and peripheral nerves. They affect several recep-
tors, including those for serotonin, norepinephrine, acetylcholine 
(leading to anticholinergic side effects), histamine (causing seda -
tion), and alpha 1-adrenergic receptors. Despite comprehensive 
analyses, including a Cochrane review, there is no strong evidence 
supporting the use of TCAs for acute LBP .45,274 While their effi -
cacy in chronic LBP is also considered weak, TCAs remain a first-
line treatment for neuropathic pain, with a number needed to 
treat as low as two.81,112 However, due to their narrow therapeutic 
window, TCAs are generally avoided in older adults due to the 
potential for side effects. Nortriptyline and desipramine are both 
secondary amines and offer better tolerance at lower doses, par -
ticularly for lumbar radicular pain. 112 These medications primar-
ily affect norepinephrine reuptake, compared with tertiary amines, 
such as amitriptyline and imipramine, which affect norepineph -
rine and serotonin more strongly.
Serotonin-Norepinephrine Reuptake Inhibitors. Duloxetine, 
an SNRI, is effective for managing both chronic musculoskeletal 
LBP and neuropathic pain, with a more favorable safety profile 
than TCAs.81,112,146 Multiple reviews have shown duloxetine to be 
superior to placebo in reducing pain and improving func -
tion.112,146,238 Studies suggest that a daily dose of 60 mg offers the 
greatest efficacy for alleviating pain and disability while minimizing 
minor adverse effects.112,146 Common side effects include nausea, 
drowsiness, insomnia, and dizziness. Although rare, transient in -
creases in liver function tests and hepatitis have been reported. If 
transaminitis develops, the medication should be discontinued.146
Gabapentinoids
Gabapentinoids, such as gabapentin and pregabalin, modulate 
voltage-dependent calcium channels within the central nervous 
system, leading to a reduction in the release of neurotransmitters 
such as glutamate, norepinephrine, substance P , and calcitonin 
gene-related peptide. Initially developed as anticonvulsants, these 
medications are now primarily used for the treatment of neuro -
pathic pain and for conditions such as lumbar radiculopathy. 
Reviews have shown that gabapentin is not effective for managing 
chronic axial LBP .274,346
Interventional Procedures
Interventional procedures have an essential role in the diagnosis 
and treatment of LBP . In general, they are minimally invasive 
techniques that require image guidance (i.e., fluoroscopy, CT , 
ultrasound) to target specific anatomic structures. The two most 
common imaging modalities used in interventional pain manage-
ment for LBP are fluoroscopy and ultrasound, with wide use of 
fluoroscopy-guided techniques and select use of ultrasound-
guided techniques depending on the region being targeted in the 
lumbosacral spine. CT-guided procedures for LBP are often per -
formed by an interventional radiologist. Interventional proce -
dures are used to alleviate pain, improve function, and enhance 
quality of life, but they are typically not curative.

735Chapter 34  Low Back Disorders
Interventional procedures for LBP are mostly performed in the 
outpatient setting with short recovery times. In most circum -
stances, patients can resume usual activities immediately or within 
a few days of the procedure. The effectiveness of interventional 
procedures for LBP varies based on the underlying condition, 
specific procedure, and contributing factors such as depression, 
poor baseline function, and obesity.
57 While these techniques 
generally have low rates of serious adverse events when performed 
by physicians with subspeciality and/or applicable training, physi-
cians should be vigilant for potential complications and prioritize 
patient safety.
211 The following section provides an overview of 
several key interventional procedures for LBP . For more detailed 
guidance in the practice and administration of interventional 
procedures, formal training and certification are advised.
Trigger Point Injections
Indications. T rigger point injections are indicated for the 
treatment of myofascial pain syndrome, characterized by the 
presence of trigger points in skeletal muscle. These injections are 
also used for chronic musculoskeletal pain, including LBP , neck 
pain, and headache disorders.
Procedural Technique. T rigger point injections focus on 
identifying and inactivating myofascial trigger points. These 
hyperirritable spots occur within taut bands of skeletal muscle 
fiber and cause localized and referred pain. The clinician pal-
pates the muscle to identify a tender nodule within a taut band 
that recreates the patient’s symptoms. Once identified, the clini -
cian can place a needle within the trigger point and perform dry 
needling or inject the location with local anesthetics or saline. 
Accurate needle placement into a trigger point can elicit a local 
twitch response. Following the trigger point injection, postinjec -
tion stretching exercises are recommended.
314
Evidence. A systematic review found trigger point injections 
to be relatively safe; however, there was no clear evidence for or 
against their effectiveness. It was suggested that the addition of 
trigger point injections to stretching exercises can augment 
treatment outcomes for patients with chronic musculoskeletal 
pain, including LBP .
311 There is evidence advising that trigger 
point injections can be performed with or without local 
anesthetic, but corticosteroid injections into trigger points 
should be avoided because supportive evidence is lacking and 
the risk of potential adverse effects is increased (i.e., local muscle 
necrosis).
220 A prospective randomized study demonstrated that 
trigger point injections were superior to NSAIDs for acute LBP 
in the emergency department setting and should be considered 
in the treatment of acute LBP .
186 The American Society of 
Regional Anesthesia and Pain Medicine, in collaboration with 
the American Society of Anesthesiologists, recommends that 
trigger point injections, as a part of a multimodal treatment 
approach, be considered for the treatment of patients with myo -
fascial pain, resulting in pain relief ranging from 1 to 4 months, 
although evidence is not definitive.
271
Lumbar Facet Joint Injections
Indications. Indications for lumbar facet joint injections in -
clude facet arthropathy, lumbar spondylosis, facet joint syndrome, 
postlaminectomy syndrome, and degeneration of the lumbar 
IVD, resulting in predominantly axial LBP .
Procedural Technique.  Image guidance is used to visualize 
the joint and advance the needle into the joint without damag-
ing neighboring structures (Fig. 34.23). Needle placement, 
sometimes identified by the feel of a pop, is confirmed with the 
injection of a small amount of contrast if fluoroscopy or CT is 
used. A corticosteroid with local anesthetic is typically included 
in the injectate.
Evidence. The evidence supporting the benefits of facet joint 
injections beyond identifying the pain generator is limited. When 
comparing patient responses to intraarticular vs. periarticular 
fluoroscopically guided facet injections, both approaches pro-
vided statistically significant immediate and short-term pain  
relief, as observed immediately and 1 week after the injections.
178 
A systematic review identified level III evidence for short-term 
improvement from lumbar intraarticular injections.
222 However, 
facet joint injections did not demonstrate an ability to reduce or 
delay the need for radiofrequency neurotomy of the medial 
branches in patients experiencing facet joint pain.
93,175,176,357
Lumbar Facet Joint Cyst Aspiration/Rupture
Indications. Lumbar facet joint cyst aspiration/rupture indica-
tions include the presence of a lumbar facet joint synovial cyst 
that results in radicular pain from compression or displacement of 
a corresponding nerve root or axial pain due to a degenerative 
facet joint. This procedure may also be warranted when neuro -
logic signs or symptoms are present.
Procedural Technique. Image guidance is used to visualize the 
joint and advance the needle into the joint without damaging 
neighboring structures ( Fig. 34.24). Needle placement, some -
times identified by the feel of a pop, is confirmed with the injec
-
tion of a small amount of contrast when fluoroscopy or CT is

aspiration, and fenestration. 319 This can be followed by intraar-
ticular injection of local anesthetic with or without corticosteroid. 
In some circumstances, an ESI is administered to target radicular 
pain following the cyst aspiration.
Evidence. There is mixed evidence for the long-term benefit of 
lumbar facet joint cyst aspiration. Multiple studies have investi -
gated symptom resolution, recurrence rates, and the need for sur-
gery. A literature review found percutaneous aspiration to have 
higher recurrence and failure rates than surgical decompression 
and cyst removal.
91 A retrospective review of the long-term effec-
tiveness of CT-guided lumbar facet joint cyst aspiration and fenes-
tration found that at mean follow-up time of 49 months, 56% of 
patients had partial or complete symptomatic relief, and 44% of 
patients underwent an operation.
315 The study suggested percuta-
neous cyst aspiration be considered prior to surgical intervention.
Lumbar Medial Branch Blocks and  
Radiofrequency Ablation
Indications. The indications for lumbar medial branch blocks 
and radiofrequency ablation include the presence of facet ar -
thropathy, lumbar spondylosis, facet joint syndrome, postlami -
nectomy syndrome, or degeneration of the lumbar IVD resulting 
in predominantly axial LBP .
Procedural Technique. Lumbar medial branch blocks and 
lumbar medial branch radiofrequency ablation are performed 
under image guidance to direct the needle to the medial branch 
of the dorsal ramus of the spinal nerve, which is located near the 
junction of the superior articular process and transverse process 
(Fig. 34.25). A small quantity of local anesthetic is used to block 
the nerve, and the patient’s response is recorded. If the patient 
obtains a significant degree of pain relief from the anesthetic 
blockade, radiofrequency ablation of the medial branch is consid-
ered. Of note, some pain societies advocate for a two-block  
paradigm to reduce the rate of false-positive results. The terms 
radiofrequency ablation and radiofrequency neurotomy are used in -
terchangeably. Radiofrequency ablation is performed by guiding a 
radiofrequency cannula tip to the location of the medial branch 
nerve. A radiofrequency probe is inserted into the cannula, using 
the generator to perform nerve stimulation, if desired. Sensory 
nerve stimulation can be performed but has not demonstrated 
significant improvement in accuracy.
59 The needle should be ad-
justed if radicular pain in the lower extremity occurs. Motor 
stimulation should demonstrate muscle activation of the multifi-
dus but not in the lower extremity. Subsequently, the generator 
delivers radiofrequency pulses to the medial branch nerve. The 
heat generated by the probe typically reaches temperatures be-
tween 60°C and 90°C (140°F to 194°F), which is sufficient to 
cause coagulation of proteins within the nerve and disrupt the 
nerve’s cellular structure. The probe is placed parallel to the nerve 
to create the largest lesion possible.
12 The effectiveness of pain 
relief depends on the duration of heat application, the tempera -
ture achieved, and the characteristics of the nerve being targeted.
Evidence. The evidence for lumbar radiofrequency ablation is 
generally favorable in terms of pain relief outcomes in well-se -
lected candidates, with discussion surrounding the degree of relief 
from medial branch blocks required (i.e., .50%, .75%, .80% 
relief) to reliably predict success from radiofrequency ablation.
55 
A systematic review of the success of initial and repeat medial 
branch radiofrequency ablation for facet joint pain indicated a 
similar pain relief duration of 7 to 9 months after initial and re -
peat radiofrequency ablation.
323 Another systematic review found
