Sacroiliac Joint Injections
Indications. The indications for sacroiliac joint injection include 
the presence of sacroiliac joint arthropathy, sacroiliac joint inflam-
mation or sacroiliitis, or sacroiliac joint dysfunction, resulting in 
sacroiliac mediated pain or LBP in the region of the sacroiliac joints. 
Of note, sacroiliac-mediated pain can result from intraarticular  
(i.e., arthritis, spondyloarthropathy) and extraarticular (ligamentous 
or muscular injury, enthesopathy) pathology.
161 Predisposing factors 
to sacroiliac-mediated pain include leg-length discrepancy, transi-
tional anatomy, scoliosis, persistent low-grade trauma or strain, 
pregnancy, and spine surgery.
56
Procedural Technique. Image guidance is used to visualize the 
joint and relevant structures. Fluoroscopy or ultrasound guidance 
is often used to advance the needle into the joint without damag-
ing neighboring structures. Correct needle placement accessing 
the inferior third of the joint is confirmed with the administration 
of a contrast agent when fluoroscopy or CT are used. A cortico-
steroid with local anesthetic is typically injected into the joint 
(Fig. 34.26). The volume of injectate varies among providers but 
is generally less than or equal to 2 mL.
Evidence. A systematic review of fluoroscopically guided sac-
roiliac joint injections found moderate-quality evidence to sup-
port the procedure. This included two randomized controlled 
trials (RCTs) and multiple observational studies supporting the 
effectiveness of sacroiliac joint injections. The value of image-
guided diagnostic injections of local anesthetics to predict positive 
responses to therapeutic agents is unclear.
174 When choosing im-
age guidance for sacroiliac joint injections, fluoroscopic guidance 
is recommended for greater accuracy in diagnostic procedures. 
However, for therapeutic purposes, ultrasound guidance is con -
sidered just as effective as fluoroscopy. Multiple studies suggest 
that the therapeutic effect of the injection does not rely on  
intraarticular needle placement because periarticular injections 
provide equal effect.
156,304
Sacral Lateral Branch Blocks and  
Radiofrequency Ablation
Indications. The indications for sacral lateral branch blocks 
and radiofrequency ablation include pain in the posterior sacro -
iliac joint complex, which is comprised of posterior sacroiliac 
ligaments and the posterior portion of the sacroiliac joint.
318,384
Procedural Technique. Sacral lateral branch blocks and sacral 
lateral branch radiofrequency ablation are performed under fluo -
roscopic guidance to direct the needle to the location of the lateral 
branch of the dorsal ramus of the spinal nerve. The posterior 
sacroiliac joint complex is innervated by the S1-S3 lateral branches 
of the dorsal rami with possible contributions from the L5 dorsal 
ramus, each of which can be a potential target. There is variability 
in the location of where lateral branch nerves exit from the dorsal 
sacral foramen, and multiple techniques are described to increase 
capture of the lateral branches (Fig. 34.27).
65,286,338 When per-
forming sacral lateral branch blocks, local anesthetic is used to 
block the nerve, and the patient’s response is recorded. If the pa-
tient obtains a significant degree of pain relief from the anesthetic 
blockade, radiofrequency ablation of the lateral branches is con -
sidered. Similar to the medial branch, radiofrequency ablation is 
performed by guiding a radiofrequency cannula tip to the loca -
tion of the lateral branch nerve. A radiofrequency probe is in -
serted into the cannula, using the generator to perform nerve 
stimulation, if desired. Sensory nerve stimulation can be per -
formed, and the needle moved if radicular pain occurs. Motor 
stimulation, if performed, should not activate muscles in the 
lower extremity. Subsequently, the generator delivers radiofre -
quency pulses to the nerve, causing denervation at specified tem -
peratures using the preferred ablative technique (i.e., conventional 
monopolar, cooled monopolar, linear bipolar strip).
286
Evidence. A meta-analysis demonstrated radiofrequency abla-
tion for sacroiliac joint pain to be an effective treatment at 3 and

joint’s anterior and posterior innervation.
181 A review found 
moderate evidence to support the efficacy and effectiveness of

739Chapter 34  Low Back Disorders
Lumbar Epidural Steroid Injections
Indications. Indications for lumbar ESIs include radiculopa -
thy, radicular pain, and neurogenic claudication, which can arise 
from disc herniation, degenerative disc disease, and foraminal or 
central spinal stenosis. Postlaminectomy syndrome, postherpetic 
neuralgia, and axial discogenic pain are also indications with vary-
ing levels of evidence.
Procedural Technique. There are three common approaches 
to access the lumbar epidural space to perform an ESI. These in -
clude the interlaminar, transforaminal, and caudal approaches 
(Figs. 34.28 through 34.30). Fluoroscopy or CT scanning can be 
used to visualize relevant anatomic structures and advance the 
needle into the epidural space without damaging neighboring 
structures. An interlaminar approach accesses the epidural space 
from the posterior spine between adjacent vertebral lamina in a 
paramedian fashion. A transforaminal approach accesses the epi -
dural space through the neuroforamen adjacent to the nerve root 
within the intervertebral foramen. A caudal approach accesses the 
epidural space through the sacral hiatus. Needle placement is 
confirmed with the injection of a small amount of contrast agent.
Evidence. A meta-analysis of RCTs of ESIs vs. conservative 
treatment for patients with lumbosacral radicular pain found the 
use of ESIs more effective than conservative treatment in the short 
term (#1 month) and intermediate term (1 to 3 months). How -
ever, this effect was not maintained at long-term follow-up  
(6 months to #1 year).
385 A systematic review and meta-analysis 
of RCTs of ESIs in the prevention of surgery for spinal pain found 
a surgery-sparing effect in the short term (, 1 year) and suggested 
ESIs may reduce the need for surgery in those who would other -
wise proceed to surgery.
24 A comprehensive review found strong 
evidence that lumbar transforaminal ESIs are an effective treat-
ment for radicular pain due to disc herniation. Although small 
studies suggest a possible benefit, there is a lack of high-quality 
evidence for the effectiveness of lumbar transforaminal ESIs for 
radicular pain due to spinal stenosis. When considering medica -
tions administered, lumbar transforaminal ESIs using nonparticu-
late steroids was as effective as particulate steroids, with the safety 
profile of nonparticulate steroids being superior.
321
Lumbar Discography
Indications. The indications for lumbar discography include a 
high index of suspicion for discogenic pain with associated pain 
severe enough to warrant surgical consideration (i.e., discectomy, 
fusion). Discography was originally used as a routine diagnostic

replaced following the development of high-quality, noninvasive 
diagnostic testing such as MRI and CT .
58 Given that disc degen-
eration is a common finding, even in asymptomatic individuals, 
advanced imaging techniques cannot determine if a degenerated 
or disrupted disc is the source of pain.
231 Provocative discography 
can be used to assess if pain complaints are concordant with diag-
nostic imaging findings and to identify which specific levels might 
benefit from further intervention.
Procedural Technique. Image guidance with fluoroscopy is 
used to visualize relevant anatomic structures and the vertebral 
level of interest. A single-needle technique or double-needle

double-needle technique is proposed to reduce the risk of con -

technique and periprocedural antibiotics can reduce the risk of 
infection regardless of technique. At the disc entry site, the 
needle is met with resistance as it contacts the annulus fibrosis, 
further advancing into the nucleus pulposus ( Fig. 34.31).  
Needle placement is confirmed with the injection of contrast 
(nucleogram). The volume of contrast injected, pain response, 
and pattern of dye distribution are carefully recorded. Pain 
provocation is a critical component in provocative discography, 
as a normal disc should not elicit pain, whereas a compromised

imaging can also be considered. Multiple criteria and standards 
qualifying a positive discogram have been described by different 
authors and groups, including Walsh,
368a Carragee, 42a Derby,76a 
International Pain and Spine Intervention Society (IPSIS; for -
merly known as Spine Intervention Society), and International 
Association for the Study of Pain (IASP).
150a These include dif-
ferences in requirements for concordant pain responses, pressur -
ization, pain behaviors, and control discs. 380
Evidence. The use of discography as a diagnostic tool for dis -
cogenic LBP is often debated, with inconsistent findings regard-
ing diagnostic utility, prognostic utility, and safety. A systematic 
review found level II evidence for the diagnostic accuracy of 
lumbar provocation discography and suggested the procedure’s 
utility for evaluating chronic lumbar discogenic pain when per -
formed under IASP criteria with control disc, minimum intensity 
of 7/10, or at least 70% reproduction of worst pain.
221 Contro-
versy surrounds discography, suggesting high false-positive rates, 
increased incidence of disc degeneration, and unnecessary surger-
ies.
41-43 A review concluded that when adhering to IPSIS/IASP 
standards, discography is associated with low false-positive rates, 
does not result in an increased incidence of clinically relevant fu-
ture disc degeneration or disruption, and suggests that a negative 
response to provocative discography can guide a patient away 
from a poorly indicated surgery.
231
Vertebral Augmentation: Vertebroplasty and Kyphoplasty
Indications. The indications for vertebral augmentation in -
clude painful VB compression fractures resulting from osteoporo-
sis, malignancy, or hemangiomas. Age-related osteoporosis is the 
most common cause of lumbar VB compression fractures. Percu-
taneous vertebral augmentation includes two techniques: vertebro-
plasty, which was developed first, and kyphoplasty (Fig. 34.32).
Procedural Technique. Image guidance with fluoroscopy is 
used to visualize relevant anatomic structures and the vertebral 
level of interest. A transpedicular approach is commonly taken to 
access the VB under intermittent multiplanar fluoroscopy. With 
vertebroplasty, once access to the VB is achieved, bone cement is 
injected to stabilize the fracture and alleviate pain. With kypho -
plasty, an additional step includes inserting a balloon percutane -
ously into the VB, which is inflated to create a cavity and restore 
VB height. The balloon is subsequently deflated and removed. 
The remaining cavity is filled with bone cement. Fluoroscopic 
images are used to confirm proper cement placement and avoid 
cement leakage.
Evidence. There has been conflicting evidence and opinion 
surrounding vertebral augmentation for VB compression fractures. 
Early studies, such as those by Kallmes and Buchbinder, ques-
tioned the efficacy of vertebroplasty compared to sham procedures, 
showing no significant difference in pain relief.
37,162 However, 
subsequent studies and meta-analyses have demonstrated

Basivertebral Nerve Ablation
Indications. The indications for basivertebral nerve ablation 
include chronic axial LBP of vertebrogenic origin, characterized 
by VB end-plate changes, often described as Modic changes iden-
tified on MRI. More specifically, indications include patients with 
axial LBP greater than 6 months in duration, refractory to conser-
vative nonsurgical management, and with evidence of type 1 and/
or type 2 Modic changes of vertebral end plates on MRI.
252,300,301
Procedure Technique. Image guidance with fluoroscopy is used 
to visualize relevant anatomic structures and the vertebral level of 
interest. A transpedicular approach includes advancing an intro-
ducer cannula through the pedicle into the VB. The introducer 
trocar is exchanged with a cannula targeting the location of the 
basivertebral nerve, located near the center of the VB (Fig. 34.33). 
The stylet is removed, and a radiofrequency probe is inserted and 
connected to the radiofrequency ablation generator. Ablation is 
then performed at 85 to 95 degrees for 15 minutes at the basiverte-
bral nerve terminus creating a 1-cm spherical lesion.
Evidence. Multiple societies support the use of basivertebral 
nerve ablation based on studies demonstrating its efficacy and 
safety.
252,300 Notably, a prospective randomized double-blind 
sham-controlled trial showed significant improvements in Oswes-
try Disability Index (ODI) and Visual Analog Scale (VAS) scores 
at 3 months postprocedure, with sustained benefits at 12 months.101 
Another study reported significant reductions in pain and disabil-
ity at 3 and 12 months, with a clinical success rate of 96.5%. 68  
A systematic review and meta-analysis found moderate-quality 
evidence supporting basivertebral nerve ablation effectiveness in 
reducing pain and disability in patients with vertebrogenic LBP .
61
Lumbar Interspinous Spacer Devices
Indications. The indications for percutaneous lumbar interspi-
nous spacer devices include symptomatic lumbar spinal stenosis 
with neurogenic claudication. These devices are particularly suit -
able for patients who have failed to improve with conservative 
management, experience symptomatic relief with lumbar flexion, 
and are looking for a minimally invasive alternative to traditional 
decompressive surgery.
Procedural Technique. Image guidance with fluoroscopy is 
used to visualize relevant anatomic structures and the vertebral 
level of interest. A small incision is made. A dilator is then intro -
duced and advanced with the use of a mallet to the spinolaminar 
line, and the interspinous spacer device is deployed between the 
spinous processes of the affected vertebral levels ( Fig. 34.34). AP 
and lateral fluoroscopic views confirm final positioning.
Evidence. A meta-analysis of RCTs found that while interspi-
nous spacer devices had similar effects as decompressive surgery for 
multiple outcomes measures (i.e., ODI score, Zurich Claudication 
Questionnaire [ZCQ] symptom severity, VAS score) and more 
favorable outcomes for others (i.e., shorter operation time, lower 
dural violation rate, ZCQ physical function score), they had 
higher reoperation rates than decompressive surgery. Both interspi-
nous spacer devices and decompressive surgery were considered 
acceptable strategies for treating symptomatic lumbar spinal steno-
sis.
382 A study of the cost effectiveness of three treatments of lum-
bar spinal stenosis compared costs and quality adjusted life years 
(QALYs) gained with minimally invasive interspinous spacer de -
vices, decompressive surgery, and conservative care. Both interspi-
nous spacer devices and decompressive surgery provided superior 
value (cost/QALY increase) vs. conservative care.
260 The MIST 
Consensus Group guidelines for minimally invasive spine treat -
ment of lumbar spinal stenosis found level I evidence in support of

Neuromodulation
Indications. T raditional spinal cord stimulation, also known as 
dorsal column stimulation, is indicated for chronic pain condi -
tions such as failed back surgery syndrome, complex regional pain 
syndrome, refractory angina pectoris, painful diabetic neuropa -
thy, and chronic radicular pain. DRG stimulation has its strongest 
indications for complex regional pain syndrome, while benefits 
have also been shown for other conditions.
46 These interventions 
are typically considered when conservative treatments have failed 
to provide adequate pain relief.
Procedure Technique. Image guidance with fluoroscopy is 
used to visualize relevant anatomic structures and the vertebral 
level of interest. The patient’s pain location and distribution dic-
tate the level of the spinal cord targeted for lead placement  
(Fig. 34.35). An epidural needle is inserted, and leads are ad -
vanced within the epidural space. For dorsal column stimulation, 
the leads are advanced and placed in a midline or paramedian 
position in proximity to the dorsal columns of the spinal cord at 
the level corresponding with pain symptoms. For LBP , leads are 
generally placed at the T8-T9 vertebral levels. For DRG stimula -
tion, leads are advanced and placed near the intervertebral fora-
men to target the DRG corresponding with pain symptoms  
(Fig. 34.36). The procedure is usually a two-step process. An ini -
tial trial period is typically conducted to assess for adequate pain 
relief, while trial leads are left in place for approximately 1 week

successful (i.e., at least a 50% reduction of target pain), an im-
plant procedure is performed where epidural leads are once again 
placed, an implantable pulse generator (IPG) is positioned subcu-
taneously, and the leads are connected to the generator. There are 
various types of IPGs, including rechargeable and nonrecharge-
able, with rechargeable IPGs having a longer lifespan.
Evidence. High-quality RCTs and systematic reviews support 
the efficacy of spinal cord stimulation for various chronic pain 
conditions. Patient selection is key, with presurgical psychological 
screening and a trial procedure recommended to optimize out-
comes and improve success rates.
72,240,316,368 A systematic review 
found level 1 evidence supporting spinal cord stimulation as a 
treatment for axial LBP , lumbar radiculopathy, neuropathic pain, 
and complex regional pain syndrome.
69 Another systematic  
review demonstrated long-term (minimum of 1-year follow-up) 
pain reduction and functional improvement in patients with 
chronic pain.
389 Studies by North and Kumar showed significant 
pain relief for subjects with pain secondary to failed back surgery 
syndrome treated with spinal cord stimulation compared to reop-
eration or conventional medical management.
194,253 Regarding 
DRG stimulation, studies have shown superior outcomes for the 
treatment of complex regional pain syndrome compared to tradi-
tional spinal cord stimulation of the dorsal columns.
71
Summary
LBP is the leading cause of disability worldwide, and understand-
ing spinal anatomy and physiology is key to pinpointing the 
source of pain along with a thorough history and physical exami -
nation. While routine imaging is not recommended for most 
cases of acute, nonspecific LBP , advanced imaging techniques 
become essential when red flags are present or symptoms persist 
without improvement. The initial treatment for acute and sub -
acute LBP focuses on patient education, physical therapy, and 
activity modification, along with appropriate medications. Ad -
dressing psychological factors is also important to prevent pain 
from becoming chronic. Exercise therapy, including stretching, 
strengthening, and aerobic conditioning, is vital in managing 
both acute and chronic LBP . For patients who do not respond to 
AB

treatment of chronic postsurgical pain secondary to triple neurectomy. Interv Pain Med. 2023;2[1]:100245.)
these conservative treatments, interventional procedures might be 
considered, though their long-term effectiveness varies. In recalci-
trant LBP , a multidisciplinary approach involving various health 
lowcare professionals and multimodal care may be required to 
improve outcomes.
